text:
Section: definition
This section is about the definition of the disease.
Apple Scab is a fungal disease caused by Venturia inaequalis that attacks apple trees in cool, moist climates. It is one of the most economically damaging diseases in apple production worldwide, capable of causing severe defoliation and rendering fruit unmarketable within a single season if left unmanaged. The disease affects leaves, blossoms, and fruit, but its most characteristic and economically destructive effect is on the fruit, which develops dark, corky, scab-like lesions that reduce quality and marketability. It is widespread in temperate apple-growing regions and is considered the primary fungal threat to commercial apple production.

Section: danger
This section is about the severity of the disease.
Apple Scab is rated as a very high danger disease. It does not kill the apple tree itself, but it can destroy the commercial value of one hundred percent of the fruit crop in a single season under cool, wet conditions. Defoliation in severe cases weakens the tree and reduces productivity in subsequent years. The speed of damage is moderate, with lesions developing over one to three weeks following infection. The economic impact is among the highest of any tree fruit disease worldwide, requiring intensive fungicide programs in susceptible regions.

Section: timeline
This section is about the timeline and spread speed of the disease.
The incubation period ranges from nine to seventeen days depending on temperature, with the fastest development around twenty degrees Celsius. The most critical infection window is from green tip through petal fall, coinciding with primary ascospore release. Once a leaf or fruit is infected, visible symptoms appear within one to two weeks. The disease spreads fastest during cool, wet spring weather coinciding with early leaf development and bloom.

Section: spread
This section is about how the disease spreads.
Apple Scab overwinters in infected leaves on the orchard floor. In spring, rainfall triggers the release of ascospores from pseudothecia that develop in overwintered leaf debris. These spores are carried by wind and rain splash to emerging leaves, blossoms, and developing fruit. Young tissue is the most vulnerable and can be infected from green tip through several weeks after petal fall. After primary infections establish, the fungus produces secondary spores called conidia from new lesions, which continue spreading through the season via rain splash. Overwintered leaf debris on the orchard floor is the single most important source of primary inoculum.

Section: pathogen
This section is about the biology of the pathogen.
Venturia inaequalis is an ascomycete fungus with two reproductive stages. The sexual stage produces ascospores inside pseudothecia that form in overwintered infected leaves. These ascospores are the primary inoculum released in spring during rainfall events. The asexual stage produces conidia from sporulating lesions on leaves and fruit during the growing season, providing secondary inoculum throughout the summer. The fungus penetrates host tissue through the cuticle and requires free water on the tissue surface for spore germination and infection. It does not persist in soil.

Section: symptoms
This section is about the symptoms of the disease.
On leaves, Apple Scab produces olive-green to brown velvety spots on the upper surface that become darker and more defined with age. Heavily infected leaves may yellow and drop prematurely, leading to defoliation in severe cases. On fruit, small, circular, olive-brown lesions appear that become dark, corky, and raised as they age. Multiple lesions can merge to cover large areas of the fruit surface. Infected fruit may crack and become deformed as it grows. On blossoms and stems, dark lesions can appear on pedicels and cause flower drop before fruit set, directly reducing yield. On shoots, infected tissue may show dark lesions that can become perennial cankers in susceptible varieties.

Section: conditions
This section is about the favorable conditions for the disease.
Apple Scab thrives in temperatures between six and twenty-four degrees Celsius, with optimum infection occurring around eighteen to twenty degrees. The fungus requires continuous leaf wetness for spore germination and infection. The Mills Table is used to determine infection risk based on the combination of temperature and hours of leaf wetness. Cool, wet spring weather with frequent rain events is the primary driver of severe epidemics.

Section: cycle
This section is about the disease cycle.
The cycle begins with the fungus overwintering in infected leaves on the orchard floor. Spring rains trigger the release of ascospores, which infect young tissue during wet periods. After an incubation period of nine to seventeen days, lesions form on leaves and developing fruit. Conidia produced from these lesions provide secondary inoculum for continued spread throughout the growing season. Infected leaves fall to the orchard floor in autumn, completing the cycle and providing inoculum for the following spring.

Section: treatment
This section is about the treatment of the disease.
Cultural management begins with the removal or burial of infected leaf debris before spring to reduce primary inoculum. Pruning to improve canopy airflow reduces humidity and leaf wetness duration. Removing water sprouts and maintaining open canopy architecture is important for drying. Planting scab-resistant varieties eliminates the need for intensive fungicide programs. Variety selection is the most sustainable long-term strategy. Resistant varieties such as Liberty, Enterprise, and Crimson Crisp provide good to excellent resistance and dramatically reduce spray requirements. Most commercial varieties including Gala, Fuji, and Honeycrisp are highly susceptible and require full spray programs.

Section: management
This section is about the management of the disease.
Chemical control should begin at green tip and continue through primary scab season, typically until two to three weeks after petal fall. Fungicide classes used include multi-site protectants such as captan and mancozeb, sterol-inhibiting triazoles such as myclobutanil and fenbuconazole, strobilurins such as trifloxystrobin, and SDHI products. Modes of action must be rotated to prevent fungicide resistance. Systemic fungicides can be used post-infection within seventy-two hours of a wetting event. In organic programs, sulfur and copper are the primary options and must be applied before infection events.

Section: actions
This section is about quick actions to take.
Before budbreak, remove or shred fallen leaf debris to reduce primary inoculum. At green tip, begin protective fungicide applications. During the primary infection period from green tip through petal fall, use infection risk models to time sprays. When first lesions appear on leaves, switch to a systemic fungicide and increase monitoring frequency. After harvest, shred or remove fallen leaves before winter.

Section: scouting
This section is about monitoring and scouting.
Scout weekly from green tip through midsummer. Focus inspection on the underside of leaves and developing fruit in the lower canopy where humidity is highest. Use weather-based infection models such as the Mills Table or RIMpro to guide spray timing. After wet periods during the primary infection window, inspect within seven to ten days for first lesions.

Section: geography
This section is about the geographic distribution of the disease.
Apple Scab is most severe in humid temperate climates, particularly in the northeastern United States, northern Europe, and New Zealand. It causes less damage in dry climates such as the interior western United States where spring rainfall is minimal.

Section: definition
This section is about the definition of the disease.
Bacterial Spot is a bacterial disease caused by Xanthomonas arboricola pv. pruni that attacks peach trees in warm, humid climates. It is the most common and economically important bacterial disease of peach and nectarine worldwide, causing lesions on leaves, fruit, and twigs. The disease is particularly damaging on fruit, where lesions reduce marketability and create entry points for secondary fungal pathogens. Severe defoliation caused by the disease weakens trees and increases susceptibility to winter injury. It is present in all major peach-producing regions and is most severe in the eastern United States where warm, rainy springs and summers are common.

Section: danger
This section is about the severity of the disease.
Bacterial Spot is rated as a high danger disease on peach. In susceptible varieties during wet seasons, fruit losses of fifty to one hundred percent have been documented. Fruit with lesions cannot be marketed fresh and are often downgraded or rejected for processing as well. Repeated severe defoliation weakens tree structure, reduces cold hardiness, and shortens productive orchard life. The combination of fruit loss, defoliation, and long-term tree decline makes this one of the most economically important diseases in peach production.

Section: timeline
This section is about the timeline and spread speed of the disease.
The incubation period is three to five days under warm conditions, which allows for multiple infection cycles within a single growing season. The disease can spread rapidly during warm, rainy periods. The most critical infection windows are during petal fall and early fruit development for fruit infections, and throughout the growing season for foliar infections. Twig infections that occur in late summer provide overwintering inoculum for the following season.

Section: spread
This section is about how the disease spreads.
Xanthomonas arboricola pv. pruni overwinters in infected twig tissue and cankers formed on current-season growth. In spring, bacteria ooze from infected twigs during wet weather and are spread by wind-driven rain to leaves and developing fruit. Secondary spread from leaf to leaf and from leaf to fruit occurs throughout the season during rain events. The bacterium enters host tissue through natural openings such as stomata and hydathodes and through wounds caused by insects, hail, or blowing sand. High winds that drive rain into open stomata or create micro-wounds are particularly effective in promoting infection.

Section: pathogen
This section is about the biology of the pathogen.
Xanthomonas arboricola pv. pruni is a gram-negative, rod-shaped bacterium that multiplies in intercellular spaces of host tissue. It produces enzymes that break down cell walls and cause the water-soaked lesions characteristic of early infection. The bacterium can survive for extended periods in infected twig tissue and spreads through water films on plant surfaces. It has a wide host range within stone fruits including plum, cherry, and apricot, though peach is among the most susceptible hosts.

Section: symptoms
This section is about the symptoms of the disease.
On leaves, Bacterial Spot causes small, water-soaked angular spots that are initially yellow-green and become brown to purple-brown with age. Lesions are typically angular because they are restricted by leaf veins. The center of older lesions often falls out, giving the leaf a shot-hole appearance. Severely infected leaves turn yellow and drop, causing premature defoliation. On fruit, small, water-soaked spots develop that become sunken, brown, and corky as they age. Lesions can reach several centimeters in diameter and may crack, creating entry points for secondary infections. Severe fruit infections cause deep pitting and cracking that renders the fruit unmarketable. On twigs, the bacterium causes dark, water-soaked spots in late summer that develop into spring cankers. These cankers are the primary overwintering site and source of primary inoculum in spring.

Section: conditions
This section is about the favorable conditions for the disease.
Bacterial Spot thrives in temperatures between twenty-four and thirty degrees Celsius, with infection possible across a range of fifteen to thirty-five degrees. The bacterium requires free water on tissue surfaces for spread and infection. Wind-driven rain is particularly effective in promoting infection. Sandy soils that cause blowing sand abrasion increase wound sites available for infection. High nitrogen fertility that promotes succulent, susceptible growth increases disease severity.

Section: cycle
This section is about the disease cycle.
The cycle begins with bacteria overwintering in infected twig tissue. In spring, bacteria ooze from infected twigs during wet weather and are spread by rain to developing leaves and fruit. Multiple infection cycles occur throughout the season during rain events. In late summer, new twig infections establish the overwintering inoculum for the following season. The disease is self-sustaining within an orchard once established unless twig infections are managed.

Section: treatment
This section is about the treatment of the disease.
Cultural management includes selecting resistant varieties where possible, pruning out infected twig tissue during dormancy, and avoiding practices that increase tree stress and susceptibility. Avoiding excessive nitrogen fertilization reduces the production of highly susceptible succulent growth. Windbreaks that reduce wind-driven rain penetration into the orchard can reduce infection rates. Drip irrigation is preferred over overhead systems to reduce leaf wetness. Variety selection is the most powerful tool available. Highly susceptible varieties such as Redhaven and Reliance suffer severe losses in wet seasons, while varieties with good resistance such as Contender and Biscoe show significantly lower damage levels.

Section: management
This section is about the management of the disease.
Chemical control relies primarily on copper-based bactericides applied from petal fall through cover sprays during the growing season. Copper applications must begin at shuck split and be maintained on a seven to ten day interval during wet weather. The number of applications required varies by season and variety susceptibility. Resistance to copper has developed in some Xanthomonas populations, so resistance management is important where repeated copper use has occurred. Oxytetracycline is also registered for use on peach in some regions and provides an alternative mode of action. No fungicide has activity against the bacterial pathogen.

Section: actions
This section is about quick actions to take.
Before bloom, prune out all visible infected twig tissue with dark cankers and destroy it. At shuck split, begin copper bactericide applications and do not allow gaps during wet periods. When first leaf lesions appear, increase spray frequency to every five to seven days during wet weather. When fruit lesions are detected, assess severity and consider economic impact on marketability. After harvest, evaluate twig infection levels and schedule dormant pruning for infected material.

Section: scouting
This section is about monitoring and scouting.
Scout trees weekly from petal fall through harvest. Examine the inner canopy and lower leaves where disease is typically found first. During and after rain events, increase inspection frequency and assess new lesion development. At harvest, assess the percentage of fruit with lesions to determine economic impact and plan management for the following season. During dormant pruning, assess the density of infected twigs as an indicator of inoculum pressure.

Section: geography
This section is about the geographic distribution of the disease.
Bacterial Spot of peach is most severe in the eastern United States, where warm, rainy summers are common. It is present in all major peach-producing regions worldwide including Europe, South America, and Asia. The disease is less damaging in dry climates such as California where summer rainfall is minimal.

Section: definition
This section is about the definition of the disease.
Bacterial Spot of pepper is a bacterial disease caused by Xanthomonas euvesicatoria that attacks pepper plants in warm, humid conditions. It is one of the most destructive bacterial diseases of both pepper and tomato, causing lesions on leaves, stems, and fruit that reduce yield and marketability. The disease thrives in conditions of warm temperatures combined with high humidity and frequent rainfall, making it a persistent problem in humid pepper-growing regions worldwide. Severe infections cause extensive defoliation that exposes fruit to sunscald and reduces the photosynthetic capacity needed for fruit development.

Section: danger
This section is about the severity of the disease.
Bacterial Spot of pepper is rated as a high danger disease. In susceptible varieties during warm, wet seasons, complete crop loss is possible. Defoliation exposes fruit to sunscald and reduces fruit size and quality. Fruit lesions directly reduce marketability for fresh market production. The disease can spread very rapidly through a field during periods of warm, rainy weather and can move from the first detected infections to widespread crop damage within one to two weeks under highly favorable conditions.

Section: timeline
This section is about the timeline and spread speed of the disease.
The incubation period is three to five days under optimal conditions of warm temperatures and high humidity. The disease can spread through an entire field within a few weeks during favorable weather because of the very short time between infection and new inoculum production. The most critical period is from transplanting through the end of the growing season, with warm, wet midsummer weather producing the most rapid spread.

Section: spread
This section is about how the disease spreads.
Xanthomonas euvesicatoria is spread primarily through wind-driven rain, overhead irrigation, and infected transplants or seed. The bacterium can survive for limited periods in soil and on plant debris. Infected transplants introduced into the field are a common source of initial inoculation. Once established, the bacterium spreads rapidly during rain events and irrigation through water splash from lesions on infected tissue to healthy tissue. It enters plants through stomata, hydathodes, and wounds. Mechanical spread through field workers and equipment moving through wet foliage also occurs.

Section: pathogen
This section is about the biology of the pathogen.
Xanthomonas euvesicatoria is a gram-negative rod-shaped bacterium closely related to the pathogen causing Bacterial Spot of tomato. Multiple races of the pathogen exist that differ in virulence on pepper varieties with specific resistance genes. The bacterium produces extracellular enzymes and effector proteins that suppress plant immunity and promote colonization of intercellular spaces in leaf and fruit tissue. It survives on infected seed and plant debris but does not persist for extended periods in soil without a host.

Section: symptoms
This section is about the symptoms of the disease.
On leaves, Bacterial Spot causes small, water-soaked circular to irregular spots that become yellow-green with age and then turn brown with yellow halos. The center of lesions may fall out as tissue dies, creating a shot-hole appearance similar to that seen on peach. Severely infected leaves turn yellow and drop, exposing fruit to sunscald. On fruit, water-soaked spots develop that become raised, brown, and rough-textured with age. Fruit lesions may crack and become corky. Secondary fungal infections often establish in bacterial lesions, further reducing fruit quality. Severely infected fruit are unmarketable. On stems, elongated water-soaked lesions can develop that may crack and cause dieback of stem tissue above the infected area.

Section: conditions
This section is about the favorable conditions for the disease.
Bacterial Spot of pepper thrives in temperatures between twenty-four and thirty degrees Celsius. The bacterium requires free water on leaf surfaces for spread and infection, making overhead irrigation and rain events the primary drivers of epidemic development. High humidity that keeps leaf surfaces wet for extended periods increases infection success. Transplants grown under warm, humid greenhouse conditions are often infected before field establishment.

Section: cycle
This section is about the disease cycle.
The cycle begins with infected seed or transplants introduced into the field, or with inoculum surviving in crop debris from a previous season. Bacteria spread from initial infection sites during rain events and irrigation. Multiple infection cycles occur throughout the season, with each cycle taking three to five days under warm conditions. Late-season infections establish inoculum that may survive in soil and debris to infect the following season, though survival outside a living host is limited.

Section: treatment
This section is about the treatment of the disease.
Cultural management includes using certified disease-free seed and transplants, practicing crop rotation away from solanaceous crops for at least two years, avoiding overhead irrigation, and removing and destroying infected plant debris at the end of the season. Purchasing transplants from reputable nurseries that use hot water seed treatment and clean production practices is essential for preventing introduction. Field workers should avoid moving through wet foliage and should wash hands and sanitize tools between fields. Variety selection can reduce but not eliminate losses. Some pepper varieties carry resistance to specific races of the pathogen, but race variation means that resistance can be overcome when a new race is introduced.

Section: management
This section is about the management of the disease.
Chemical control relies primarily on copper-based bactericides applied on a seven to ten day schedule during warm, wet weather. Applications should begin at transplanting or at the first sign of disease and be maintained throughout favorable weather periods. Resistance to copper has developed in many Xanthomonas populations and is widespread in some regions, making copper applications less reliable than in the past. Where copper resistance is confirmed, rotating or combining copper with mancozeb can provide some improvement. No bactericide eliminates established infections; chemical control is purely protective.

Section: actions
This section is about quick actions to take.
Before transplanting, inspect transplants for lesions and reject any showing symptoms. At transplanting, apply a protective copper bactericide. During warm, wet weather, maintain seven-day spray intervals without exception. When first lesions appear on leaves, apply copper immediately and reduce overhead irrigation. When fruit lesions are detected, assess economic impact and adjust harvest timing if possible to recover value before further deterioration.

Section: scouting
This section is about monitoring and scouting.
Scout fields twice weekly from transplanting through harvest during warm, wet seasons. Inspect the lower canopy and undersides of leaves where disease is typically first detected. Examine transplants at establishment for early lesions. After rain events or irrigation, increase inspection frequency to detect new infections within the three to five day incubation window. Track weather conditions and apply bactericides proactively before rain events when disease is present in the field.

Section: geography
This section is about the geographic distribution of the disease.
Bacterial Spot of pepper is most severe in warm, humid pepper-growing regions including the southeastern United States, parts of Latin America, and humid regions of Europe and Asia. It is present wherever peppers are grown commercially in warm climates and is one of the primary constraints to pepper production in humid tropical and subtropical regions worldwide.

Section: definition
This section is about the definition of the disease.
Bacterial Spot of tomato is a bacterial disease caused by Xanthomonas perforans and related Xanthomonas species that attack tomato plants in warm, humid conditions. It is one of the most economically significant bacterial diseases of tomato worldwide, affecting leaves, stems, and fruit. The disease causes defoliation, which reduces fruit quality by exposing fruit to sunscald, and causes direct fruit lesions that significantly reduce marketability. It is a primary concern in commercial fresh-market tomato production in humid regions and is present wherever tomatoes are grown commercially in warm climates.

Section: danger
This section is about the severity of the disease.
Bacterial Spot of tomato is rated as a high danger disease. In highly susceptible varieties during warm, wet seasons, complete loss of marketable fruit is possible. Defoliation is the primary driver of economic loss, as exposed fruit suffers sunscald and the plant loses photosynthetic capacity needed for fruit fill. Fruit lesions directly reduce grade and value for fresh market production. The disease can spread extremely rapidly during periods of warm, rainy weather, moving from first detected infections to widespread damage within one to two weeks.

Section: timeline
This section is about the timeline and spread speed of the disease.
The incubation period is three to five days under warm, humid conditions. The very short incubation period allows for rapid epidemic development when weather is favorable. The disease is most active during warm summer periods with frequent rainfall or irrigation events. Infections can occur continuously throughout the growing season under favorable conditions, making management a continuous commitment once disease is established in the field.

Section: spread
This section is about how the disease spreads.
Xanthomonas perforans spreads primarily through wind-driven rain, overhead irrigation, infected transplants, and contaminated seed. The bacterium can survive for limited periods in soil and plant debris. Infected transplants are a common source of initial field introduction and represent the most critical point of prevention. Once established, the bacterium spreads through water splash from infected to healthy tissue during rain events and irrigation. Mechanical spread through field workers, equipment, and insects moving through wet foliage also contributes to within-field spread.

Section: pathogen
This section is about the biology of the pathogen.
Xanthomonas perforans is a gram-negative rod-shaped bacterium and the predominant species causing Bacterial Spot of tomato in the southeastern United States and many other regions. Multiple races exist that differ in virulence on varieties with specific resistance genes. The pathogen produces effector proteins that suppress plant immune responses and facilitate colonization. Race populations have shifted in response to variety resistance gene deployment, requiring continuous monitoring of race composition in commercial production areas.

Section: symptoms
This section is about the symptoms of the disease.
On leaves, Bacterial Spot causes small, water-soaked circular spots that enlarge to become irregular, brown lesions with yellow halos. Lesion centers may fall out as tissue dies, creating a ragged, shot-hole appearance. Severely infected leaves turn yellow and drop, exposing fruit and reducing photosynthetic capacity. On fruit, small, slightly raised, water-soaked spots develop that become brown, rough, and scab-like with age. Lesions are typically superficial but make fruit unmarketable for fresh sale. Secondary fungal pathogens often colonize bacterial lesions, causing additional damage. On stems and petioles, water-soaked elongated lesions may develop that cause tissue collapse and dieback above the infected area.

Section: conditions
This section is about the favorable conditions for the disease.
Bacterial Spot of tomato thrives in temperatures between twenty-four and thirty degrees Celsius and requires free water on leaf surfaces for spread and infection. Wind-driven rain and overhead irrigation are the most effective means of disease spread. High humidity that maintains leaf wetness for extended periods promotes rapid epidemic development. Transplants established under warm, humid conditions are highly susceptible to infection during the establishment period.

Section: cycle
This section is about the disease cycle.
The cycle begins with infected seed or transplants introduced into the field, or with inoculum surviving in crop debris from a previous season. Bacteria spread from initial infections during rain events and irrigation throughout the growing season. Each infection cycle takes three to five days under warm conditions, allowing for rapid epidemic buildup. Crop debris infected at the end of the season may provide limited inoculum for the following season, though survival outside a living host is limited.

Section: treatment
This section is about the treatment of the disease.
Cultural management includes using certified pathogen-free seed treated with hot water and certified disease-free transplants, implementing crop rotation with non-solanaceous crops for at least two years, switching to drip irrigation, and destroying crop debris at the end of the season. Inspection of transplants before field establishment is essential for preventing introduction. Field hygiene including washing hands and sanitizing equipment between fields reduces mechanical spread. Variety selection can reduce losses in regions where specific races predominate, but race shifts in pathogen populations have eroded the usefulness of single resistance genes. Varieties with multiple resistance genes or quantitative resistance provide more durable protection.

Section: management
This section is about the management of the disease.
Chemical control relies on copper-based bactericides applied on a five to seven day schedule during warm, wet weather. Copper applications must begin at transplanting and be maintained throughout the season during favorable weather. Copper resistance is widespread in Xanthomonas populations in many production regions, reducing the efficacy of copper alone. Adding mancozeb to copper programs improves efficacy by reducing the rate of copper resistance development and providing an additional protective mode of action. Biological bactericides based on Bacillus subtilis or Pseudomonas fluorescens are available for use in conventional and organic programs as supplementary tools.

Section: actions
This section is about quick actions to take.
Before transplanting, inspect transplants for lesions and reject symptomatic plants. At transplanting, apply protective copper bactericide. During warm, wet periods, maintain five to seven day spray intervals. When first lesions appear on leaves, apply copper immediately, reduce overhead irrigation, and avoid working in wet foliage. When fruit lesions are detected, assess economic impact and adjust harvest schedule to recover value from affected fruit before further deterioration.

Section: scouting
This section is about monitoring and scouting.
Scout fields twice weekly from transplanting through harvest during warm, wet seasons. Examine the lower canopy and undersides of leaves for early lesions. Inspect transplants at establishment for initial infections. After rain events or irrigation, inspect within three to five days for new infections given the short incubation period. Track weather conditions and apply bactericides before anticipated rain events when disease is present.

Section: geography
This section is about the geographic distribution of the disease.
Bacterial Spot of tomato is most severe in the warm, humid tomato-producing regions of the southeastern United States, parts of Latin America, and humid regions of Europe and Asia. It is present wherever tomatoes are grown commercially in warm climates and is particularly problematic in Florida, where it is a primary disease concern throughout the growing season.

Section: definition
This section is about the definition of the disease.
Black Rot is a fungal disease caused by Guignardia bidwellii that attacks grapevines in warm, humid climates. It is one of the most economically damaging diseases in viticulture, capable of destroying an entire harvest within a single season if left unmanaged. The disease affects leaves, shoots, and berries, but its most devastating and characteristic effect is on the fruit, which turns completely black and shrivels into hard, mummified balls that remain attached to the cluster. It is widespread in humid grape-growing regions worldwide and is considered a primary threat to European wine grape varieties.

Section: danger
This section is about the severity of the disease.
Black Rot is rated as a very high danger disease. It does not kill the grapevine itself, but it can destroy one hundred percent of the fruit crop in a single season under warm, wet conditions. Yield losses of this magnitude have been documented in unmanaged vineyards. The speed of damage is fast once infection begins, with berries moving from healthy to fully mummified within one to two weeks. The economic impact is among the highest of any grape disease worldwide, affecting both fresh market and wine grape production.

Section: timeline
This section is about the timeline and spread speed of the disease.
The incubation period ranges from eight to twenty-five days depending on temperature, with the fastest development at around twenty-six degrees Celsius. The most critical infection window is from bloom through four weeks after bloom, during which young berries are highly susceptible. Once a berry is infected, visible symptoms appear within one to three weeks, and mummification follows within another one to two weeks. The disease spreads fastest during warm, wet spring and early summer weather coinciding with the post-bloom period.

Section: spread
This section is about how the disease spreads.
Black Rot overwinters in mummified berries and infected canes left from the previous season. In spring, rainfall triggers the release of ascospores from overwintering structures in the old mummies and infected wood. These spores are carried by wind and rain splash to young leaves, shoots, and developing berries. Young berries are the most vulnerable tissue and can be infected from bloom until about four weeks after bloom. After primary infections establish, the fungus produces secondary spores called conidia from new lesions, which continue spreading through the season via wind and rain. Mummified berries left on the vine or on the ground are the single most important source of inoculum for the following season.

Section: pathogen
This section is about the biology of the pathogen.
Guignardia bidwellii is an ascomycete fungus with two reproductive stages. The sexual stage produces ascospores inside overwintering structures called pseudothecia, which form in mummified berries and infected canes. These ascospores are the primary inoculum released in spring during rainfall. The asexual stage produces conidia from pycnidia that form in leaf lesions during the growing season, providing secondary inoculum throughout the summer. The fungus penetrates host tissue through the cuticle and stomata and requires free water on the tissue surface for infection. It does not persist in soil.

Section: symptoms
This section is about the symptoms of the disease.
On leaves, Black Rot produces small yellowish spots that enlarge into reddish-brown circular lesions between two and ten millimeters in diameter. Each lesion has a dark brown border and contains small black pycnidia arranged in concentric rings inside the lesion. Severely infected leaves may drop prematurely. On shoots and petioles, the fungus causes dark brown to black elongated lesions that become sunken and shriveled. These can girdle and kill young shoots when infection is severe. On berries, small white spots appear first, then the berry turns entirely brown and then black. The infected berry shrivels into a hard, black mummified form that remains attached to the cluster. This mummification is the hallmark of Black Rot and is unmistakable once recognized. On cluster stems and tendrils, dark shrunken lesions can cut off water supply to entire bunches, accelerating berry collapse.

Section: conditions
This section is about the favorable conditions for the disease.
Black Rot thrives in temperatures between ten and thirty-two degrees Celsius, with the optimum around twenty-six degrees. The fungus requires a minimum of four hours of free water on the tissue surface to complete infection. At twenty-six degrees this threshold is met after just six hours of wetness, while at ten degrees it requires twenty-four hours. The disease is most severe during warm, humid summers with frequent rainfall, especially when these conditions coincide with the bloom and post-bloom period.

Section: cycle
This section is about the disease cycle.
The cycle begins with the fungus overwintering inside mummified berries and infected canes. Spring rains trigger the release of ascospores, which infect young tissue during wet periods. After an incubation of eight to twenty-five days, lesions form on leaves and shoots. Pycnidia within these lesions release conidia that spread through wind and rain for secondary infections throughout the season. Berries become infected primarily during the critical post-bloom window. Infected berries mummify and either remain on the vine or fall to the ground, carrying the fungus into the next season.

Section: treatment
This section is about the treatment of the disease.
Cultural management begins with the removal and destruction of all mummified berries during dormant pruning. This single action has the greatest impact on reducing next season's inoculum. Infected canes should also be removed. Canopy airflow should be improved through shoot positioning, leaf removal, and proper training systems to reduce humidity and accelerate drying after rain. Overhead irrigation should be avoided, and fallen leaf material should be cleared from the vineyard floor after harvest. Variety selection plays a significant role. American grape varieties such as Concord and Niagara have moderate natural resistance, while most European Vitis vinifera varieties including Chardonnay, Merlot, and Cabernet Sauvignon are highly susceptible. Disease-resistant hybrid varieties such as Marquette and Frontenac offer good to excellent resistance and are valuable where variety flexibility exists.

Section: management
This section is about the management of the disease.
Chemical control should begin at budbreak with a protectant fungicide and continue on a seven-day interval from bloom through four weeks post-bloom. This is the most critical spray window and should not be skipped. After the critical window, intervals can extend to ten to fourteen days in dry weather. Fungicide classes used include multi-site protectants such as mancozeb and captan, systemic triazoles such as myclobutanil and tebuconazole, strobilurins such as azoxystrobin, and SDHI products such as boscalid. Modes of action must be rotated every two to three sprays to prevent fungicide resistance. Strobilurins should never be used alone and should always be mixed with a protectant. In organic programs, copper-based fungicides and captan are the primary options and must be applied on tight schedules given their purely protective activity.

Section: actions
This section is about quick actions to take.
If no symptoms are present before bloom, apply a protective fungicide and remove any old mummies immediately. During the bloom period in wet weather, spray every seven days without exception. When first leaf spots appear, spray immediately and inspect all berries closely. When first infected berries are spotted, remove the infected clusters and spray remaining clusters with a systemic fungicide. After harvest, remove all mummies from vines and ground and destroy them.

Section: scouting
This section is about monitoring and scouting.
Scout weekly from budbreak through six weeks post-bloom. Focus inspection on berries, leaf undersides, and shoot tips. Assess how thoroughly mummies were removed before the season, as incompletely cleaned vineyards carry dramatically higher risk. After rain events during the critical window, inspect within three to five days for the first leaf lesions. Use disease forecasting models to optimize spray timing where available.

Section: geography
This section is about the geographic distribution of the disease.
Black Rot is most severe in humid continental climates, particularly in the eastern United States and central Europe. It is present wherever grapevines are grown commercially but causes less damage in dry Mediterranean and Pacific Coast climates where warm, wet spring conditions are less common.

Section: definition
This section is about the definition of the disease.
Black Rot is a fungal disease caused by Botryosphaeria obtusa that attacks apple trees in warm, humid climates. It is a significant disease in apple production, causing fruit rot, leaf spot, and cankers on branches. The disease affects leaves, fruit, and woody tissue, but its most economically damaging effect is on the fruit, which develops a characteristic rotting pattern with concentric rings of brown and black that eventually consumes the entire fruit. Infected fruit shrivel into black mummified forms that remain on the tree. It is widespread in humid apple-growing regions and is capable of causing severe losses in unmanaged or stressed orchards.

Section: danger
This section is about the severity of the disease.
Black Rot of apple is rated as a high danger disease. Unlike its grape counterpart, it rarely destroys an entire crop in one season under normal conditions, but it can cause significant fruit losses and persistent canker problems that weaken or kill limbs over multiple seasons. Fruit losses of thirty to fifty percent have been recorded in neglected orchards. The combination of fruit rot, foliar disease, and wood cankers makes it a multifaceted threat that can compound over time if not managed.

Section: timeline
This section is about the timeline and spread speed of the disease.
The incubation period on fruit ranges from one to three weeks depending on temperature and fruit maturity. The most critical infection window for fruit is from petal fall through the summer growing season, with late-season infections often going undetected until storage. Canker infections can occur throughout the growing season whenever wounded or stressed tissue is present. The disease spreads fastest during warm, wet summer weather.

Section: spread
This section is about how the disease spreads.
Black Rot overwinters in mummified fruit, cankers on branches, and infected wood debris. In spring and throughout the growing season, rainfall triggers the release of conidia and ascospores from these sources. Spores are spread by wind and rain splash to leaves, fruit, and wounded wood. Fruit infections typically enter through wounds, lenticels, or the calyx end. The fungus is also an opportunistic pathogen that readily infects trees stressed by winter injury, fire blight, or mechanical damage. Mummified fruit left on the tree or on the ground are important sources of inoculum.

Section: pathogen
This section is about the biology of the pathogen.
Botryosphaeria obtusa is an ascomycete fungus that produces both sexual ascospores and asexual conidia. Fruiting bodies form in overwintered canker tissue and mummified fruit and release spores during wet weather throughout the growing season. The fungus can persist for multiple years in canker tissue on living branches, making it a chronic source of inoculum within the orchard. It is also a weak pathogen that readily infects stressed or wounded tissue but can infect healthy fruit under favorable conditions.

Section: symptoms
This section is about the symptoms of the disease.
On fruit, Black Rot begins as a small brown spot that expands rapidly into a firm rot with characteristic concentric rings of alternating brown and black. Eventually the entire fruit turns black and shrinks into a mummified form. Pycnidia are visible as small black dots on the fruit surface. On leaves, the disease causes frog-eye leaf spot, circular spots with purple margins and tan or brown centers. These spots are characteristic and help distinguish the disease from other leaf diseases. On branches, the fungus causes oval to elongated cankers that are brown or reddish-brown and may ooze during wet weather. Cankers enlarge slowly and can girdle and kill limbs over several years.

Section: conditions
This section is about the favorable conditions for the disease.
Black Rot thrives in temperatures between twenty-four and thirty degrees Celsius, with infection occurring across a wide range. Warm, wet summer weather favors rapid fruit rot development. Trees stressed by drought, winter injury, insect damage, or fire blight are significantly more susceptible. Poorly pruned orchards with abundant dead wood provide ideal overwintering habitat for the pathogen.

Section: cycle
This section is about the disease cycle.
The cycle begins with the fungus overwintering in mummified fruit, cankers, and dead wood. Spores are released during wet weather from spring through the end of the growing season. Leaves are infected early in the season, and fruit infections occur from petal fall onward. Infected fruit develop visible rot symptoms within one to three weeks and eventually mummify. Canker infections expand slowly throughout the season and continue in subsequent years.

Section: treatment
This section is about the treatment of the disease.
Cultural management is central to control. All mummified fruit must be removed from trees and the orchard floor before spring. Dead wood, cankers, and fire blight strikes should be pruned out and destroyed. Wounds from pruning or storm damage should be minimized and made during dry weather when possible. Maintaining tree health through proper nutrition and irrigation reduces susceptibility. Canopy pruning to improve airflow and light penetration reduces humidity and accelerates drying.

Section: management
This section is about the management of the disease.
Chemical control focuses on protecting fruit from petal fall through harvest. Fungicide classes used include captan as a broad-spectrum protectant, triazoles such as myclobutanil and tebuconazole, and strobilurins. Applications during summer should be timed to cover periods of warm, wet weather. Fungicide rotation is important to prevent resistance. Canker control relies primarily on cultural practices, as fungicides do not cure established canker infections.

Section: actions
This section is about quick actions to take.
Before budbreak, remove all mummies and dead wood from trees and the orchard floor. At petal fall, begin protective fungicide applications. When frog-eye leaf spots appear, assess canker severity and schedule pruning. When fruit rot is found, remove infected fruit immediately and apply a systemic fungicide. After harvest, remove remaining mummies and conduct thorough dormant pruning of cankers.

Section: scouting
This section is about monitoring and scouting.
Scout weekly from petal fall through harvest. Examine fruit carefully in the lower and inner canopy where disease is often first detected. Assess canker presence on limbs during dormant pruning. After harvest, survey mummy levels on trees and on the ground to gauge inoculum pressure for the following season.

Section: geography
This section is about the geographic distribution of the disease.
Black Rot of apple is most severe in the eastern United States, where warm, humid summers favor disease development. It is present in apple-growing regions worldwide but is less damaging in dry climates where summer rainfall is limited.

Section: definition
This section is about the definition of the disease.
Cedar Apple Rust is a fungal disease caused by Gymnosporangium juniperi-virginianae that requires two different host plants to complete its life cycle: eastern red cedar or other juniper species and apple or crabapple. It is a significant disease in apple-producing regions where juniper hosts are present, causing bright orange lesions on leaves and fruit that reduce photosynthesis, cause premature defoliation, and render fruit unmarketable. The disease is unique among apple diseases in requiring an alternate host to complete its biological cycle, making proximity to juniper trees a key risk factor.

Section: danger
This section is about the severity of the disease.
Cedar Apple Rust is rated as a moderate to high danger disease. It rarely destroys an entire crop but can cause significant defoliation in susceptible varieties, weakening trees and reducing productivity. Heavy fruit infection renders apples unmarketable due to distortion and surface lesions. The severity of the disease in any given year depends heavily on the proximity of juniper hosts, spring weather conditions, and variety susceptibility. Repeated annual defoliation of susceptible trees can reduce long-term orchard productivity.

Section: timeline
This section is about the timeline and spread speed of the disease.
Infection of apple tissue occurs exclusively during a narrow window in spring corresponding to the release of basidiospores from orange gelatinous telial horns on infected cedars. This window typically coincides with apple bloom and early leaf development. The incubation period on apple is eight to fourteen days. Secondary spread from apple to apple does not occur; the fungus must return to a juniper host to complete its cycle. The entire apple infection season is typically compressed into a two to four week period in spring.

Section: spread
This section is about how the disease spreads.
The fungus overwinters as galls on eastern red cedar and other junipers. In spring, during wet weather, the galls produce bright orange gelatinous telial horns that release basidiospores. These spores are windborne and can travel significant distances, up to several kilometers, to infect apple leaves and fruit. Infected apple tissue later produces spores that infect junipers in late summer, completing the cycle. Apple tissue cannot infect other apple trees, and juniper tissue cannot infect other junipers. The alternating host requirement is an absolute biological necessity for the pathogen.

Section: pathogen
This section is about the biology of the pathogen.
Gymnosporangium juniperi-virginianae is a basidiomycete rust fungus with an obligate two-host life cycle. On juniper, it forms brown woody galls that produce bright orange telial horns during spring rains. Basidiospores released from these horns infect apple tissue. On apple, the fungus produces pycnia and aecia on leaves and fruit. Aeciospores produced on apple infect juniper tissue in late summer, where new galls develop over one to two years before the cycle repeats. The fungus cannot complete its life cycle on a single host.

Section: symptoms
This section is about the symptoms of the disease.
On apple leaves, Cedar Apple Rust causes bright yellow-orange spots on the upper surface that are highly distinctive and visible from a distance. As lesions age, small orange tube-like structures called aecia develop on the lower leaf surface directly beneath the upper lesion. Severely infected leaves may drop prematurely. On apple fruit, bright orange to yellow lesions develop on the surface, often near the calyx end. Infected fruit become distorted and are unmarketable. Aecia may also develop on fruit surfaces. On junipers, round brown galls form on branches that can range from pea-sized to golf-ball-sized. In spring, the galls produce bright orange gelatinous telial horns that are unmistakable and highly visible during wet weather.

Section: conditions
This section is about the favorable conditions for the disease.
Cedar Apple Rust requires wet, cool to mild spring weather coinciding with the period when telial horns are active on cedar galls. Infection of apple occurs most readily at temperatures between ten and twenty-one degrees Celsius with leaf wetness periods of at least four hours. Orchards situated near or downwind of large stands of eastern red cedar or ornamental junipers face the highest risk. The disease is less severe in years with dry spring weather during the bloom period.

Section: cycle
This section is about the disease cycle.
The cycle begins with mature galls on junipers that have developed over one to two seasons. During spring rains, telial horns emerge and release basidiospores that infect young apple leaves and fruit. Lesions develop on apple tissue over two to four weeks. In midsummer, aeciospores produced on the underside of apple leaves are released and carried by wind to infect juniper tissue, where new galls begin forming. These galls mature over the following one to two years before the cycle repeats.

Section: treatment
This section is about the treatment of the disease.
Removing juniper hosts from the vicinity of the orchard is the most effective long-term cultural control measure but is often impractical given the wide dispersal of basidiospores. Selecting rust-resistant apple varieties is the most sustainable management strategy where variety flexibility exists. Variety selection is critical. Resistant varieties such as Liberty, Redfree, and Enterprise have excellent resistance and require little or no fungicide for rust control. Highly susceptible varieties include Golden Delicious, Jonathan, and Rome.

Section: management
This section is about the management of the disease.
Chemical control focuses on protecting apple tissue during the primary infection window from pink bud through first cover. Fungicide applications must be timed to cover the period of basidiospore release from cedar galls. Effective fungicide classes include triazoles such as myclobutanil and fenbuconazole, and strobilurins such as trifloxystrobin. Applications should begin at pink bud and continue through petal fall on a seven to ten day schedule during wet weather. Protectant fungicides alone are less effective given the narrow and sometimes unpredictable infection window; systemic fungicides with kickback activity are preferred.

Section: actions
This section is about quick actions to take.
In late winter and early spring, inspect nearby juniper trees for brown galls to assess inoculum pressure. When telial horns become visible on cedars during spring rains, begin fungicide applications on apple immediately. At pink bud and bloom, maintain fungicide coverage without gaps during wet weather. When first orange spots appear on leaves, apply a systemic fungicide within forty-eight hours. After harvest, assess defoliation severity and note juniper gall density for planning the following season.

Section: scouting
This section is about monitoring and scouting.
Monitor nearby juniper trees from late winter for gall development. Once galls are identified, track weather conditions in spring for the onset of telial horn production. Scout apple leaves and fruit weekly from pink bud through petal fall. The window of active spore release from cedars can be tracked using online rust prediction tools available in several eastern states.

Section: geography
This section is about the geographic distribution of the disease.
Cedar Apple Rust is most severe in the eastern United States where eastern red cedar is abundant. It is present wherever susceptible apple varieties and juniper hosts occur in proximity, including parts of Canada and isolated regions where ornamental junipers are planted near orchards. The disease does not occur in regions where juniper alternate hosts are absent.

Section: definition
This section is about the definition of the disease.
Common Rust is a fungal disease of corn caused by Puccinia sorghi, a rust fungus that produces distinctive brick-red powdery pustules scattered across both surfaces of leaves. It is one of the most widespread corn diseases globally, present wherever corn is grown. While it appears alarming due to the vivid coloration of the pustules, it is rarely devastating on field corn when resistant hybrids are used. On sweet corn and highly susceptible hybrids, however, it can cause significant defoliation and yield loss. The pathogen is an obligate parasite that requires living host tissue to survive and cannot persist in soil or dead debris.

Section: danger
This section is about the severity of the disease.
The danger level of Common Rust is moderate on field corn and rises to high on sweet corn and susceptible hybrids. The disease rarely kills plants directly but reduces photosynthesis and impairs grain fill. Yield losses on susceptible field corn hybrids typically range from five to twenty percent, while losses on sweet corn can reach forty percent. Resistant field corn varieties experience minimal loss. The economic impact is moderate on field corn and high on sweet corn and popcorn. Once infected, tissue cannot be cured and retains pustules for the rest of the season.

Section: timeline
This section is about the timeline and spread speed of the disease.
Under optimal cool and humid conditions, Common Rust takes seven to fourteen days from infection to visible symptoms. The fungus produces new spore generations every seven to ten days, allowing the disease to cycle rapidly through a field. From the first pustules to severe infection takes two to four weeks in cool, humid weather. Spread stops quickly when temperatures become hot and dry, which is an important characteristic that distinguishes it from more aggressive diseases.

Section: spread
This section is about how the disease spreads.
Common Rust spreads almost exclusively through wind-dispersed urediospores that travel from infected fields, often originating hundreds of kilometers away in tropical or subtropical regions where the fungus survives year-round. In temperate climates, the fungus cannot overwinter on corn residue and must be reintroduced each season from warmer areas. Once spores land on corn leaves, they germinate in the presence of moisture, penetrate through stomata without requiring any wound, and establish infection. Within a field, each pustule produces millions of new spores that spread to neighboring plants through wind. Rotation and tillage do not reduce risk because inoculum arrives from distant sources, not from local residue.

Section: pathogen
This section is about the biology of the pathogen.
Puccinia sorghi is a basidiomycete rust fungus with a complex life cycle involving two hosts. On corn, it produces urediospores that drive epidemic spread and teliospores that form late in the season. Teliospores infect the alternate host, Oxalis species or wood sorrel, on which sexual recombination produces aeciospores that can re-infect corn. In tropical and subtropical regions this full cycle maintains year-round populations. In temperate regions the alternate host cycle is less epidemiologically important. The fungus is an obligate biotroph, meaning it grows only in living tissue and cannot be cultured on artificial media. This makes it difficult to study in the laboratory but also means it dies with its host in cold climates.

Section: symptoms
This section is about the symptoms of the disease.
On leaves, Common Rust produces small, circular to elongate pustules that are brick-red to cinnamon-brown in color and appear on both the upper and lower leaf surfaces. This bilateral distribution is an important diagnostic feature that distinguishes Common Rust from Southern Rust, which produces pustules mainly on the upper surface. The pustules rupture the leaf epidermis and release a powdery mass of brick-red urediospores. Later in the season, pustules darken to brown or black as teliospores replace urediospores. In severe infections, leaves turn yellow and then brown and die prematurely. Sheaths and husks may also be infected in very susceptible hybrids under heavy pressure.

Section: conditions
This section is about the favorable conditions for the disease.
Common Rust thrives in cool temperatures between sixteen and twenty-three degrees Celsius, with the optimum around twenty degrees. Dew, fog, or rain on leaf surfaces is required for spore germination. The disease is most severe from the V10 growth stage through silking, and late-planted corn is at highest risk because it passes through the susceptible stage when inoculum levels are at their peak. High-elevation tropical corn production is particularly vulnerable. Cool nights with heavy dew are a strong predictor of active infection.

Section: cycle
This section is about the disease cycle.
Each season in temperate regions, urediospores arrive via wind from southern or tropical areas. They land on corn leaves, germinate in moisture, and penetrate through stomata. Pustules develop within seven to fourteen days and release new urediospores. Multiple infection cycles repeat throughout the season, each cycle seven to ten days long. Late in the season, dark teliospores form within pustules. In regions where both A1 and A2 mating types coexist, sexual recombination on the Oxalis alternate host generates new races capable of overcoming host resistance genes.

Section: treatment
This section is about the treatment of the disease.
The most effective management strategy is planting hybrids with strong rust resistance ratings. Hybrids rated six or above on a nine-point scale tolerate substantial rust pressure without significant yield loss. Quantitative or partial resistance, which is controlled by multiple genes, is more durable than single-gene resistance because it is harder for new fungal races to overcome completely. Cultural practices have limited value against Common Rust because inoculum arrives from wind rather than from local fields. Planting early allows the crop to pass through the most susceptible vegetative stages before peak inoculum arrives. Removing volunteer corn plants reduces early-season infection sources. Balanced nutrition, particularly adequate phosphorus and potassium, supports plant tolerance.

Section: management
This section is about the management of the disease.
Fungicide applications are economically justified on susceptible hybrids and sweet corn when disease reaches the ear leaf or above at or before tasseling. Effective fungicide classes include triazoles such as propiconazole and tebuconazole, strobilurins such as azoxystrobin and pyraclostrobin, and premix products combining SDHI and triazole chemistries. The optimal timing is between the VT and R1 growth stages. A single well-timed application is usually sufficient. On sweet corn the threshold is much lower and fungicide should be applied as soon as rust appears before or at tasseling. Applications after the R3 milk stage have very limited economic return on field corn. Modes of action should be alternated to reduce resistance risk. Biological control options currently have minimal practical value against corn rust. Bacillus-based biofungicides show little activity, and no commercially effective biological product exists for this disease.

Section: actions
This section is about quick actions to take.
Before planting, choose a hybrid with a rust resistance rating of six or higher out of nine. During early vegetative stages when pustules are present, monitor closely but do not spray because plants can tolerate early rust without economic loss. When approaching tasseling with rust visible on the ear leaf, apply a triazole or premix fungicide immediately. On sweet corn at any stage with rust visible, apply fungicide promptly because sweet corn tolerates much less pressure. After tasseling in a severely infected field, apply fungicide only if the crop is before the R3 dough stage.

Section: scouting
This section is about monitoring and scouting.
Begin scouting at the V8 growth stage and intensify from V10 through R2. Sample at least twenty plants per field at multiple locations throughout the field. Record the percentage of plants showing rust and note the highest leaf position where pustules are present. The key threshold for fungicide application is disease on the ear leaf or above at tasseling. Monitor weather for cool nights below twenty degrees Celsius combined with dew, which signals high infection risk. Regional rust forecasting maps are available in some areas and can guide scouting priorities.

Section: geography
This section is about the geographic distribution of the disease.
Common Rust is present globally wherever corn is grown. It is most severe in tropical highlands such as East Africa, Central America, and the Andean region of South America. In temperate regions of North America and Europe, severity varies year to year depending on how early inoculum arrives from southern source regions and whether cool, humid weather follows. Late-planted fields and high-elevation areas within any region are at the greatest risk.

Section: definition
This section is about the definition of the disease.
Gray Leaf Spot is one of the most yield-limiting fungal diseases of corn worldwide, caused by Cercospora zeae-maydis. It produces long, rectangular, gray to tan lesions that run parallel to leaf veins, eventually causing entire leaves to blight and die. The disease is particularly destructive in no-till production systems where infected corn residue remains on the soil surface as the primary source of inoculum season after season. It thrives in warm, humid environments with prolonged overnight dew and poor air circulation through the canopy. Severe infections can destroy fifty percent or more of yield by killing the leaf canopy before grain fill is complete, and they also predispose plants to serious stalk rot at harvest.

Section: danger
This section is about the severity of the disease.
Gray Leaf Spot is rated as a high-danger disease. It does not kill plants directly, but severe defoliation before grain fill is complete prevents ears from developing normally and causes premature plant death through stalk rot. Yield losses range from twenty to fifty percent in severe cases, and losses exceeding fifty percent have been recorded in highly susceptible hybrids under ideal disease conditions. The disease reduces grain weight, test weight, and stalk integrity at harvest. Once lesions form, fungicides cannot reverse existing damage but can protect healthy remaining tissue.

Section: timeline
This section is about the timeline and spread speed of the disease.
Infection to visible symptoms takes fourteen to twenty-one days under optimal warm, humid conditions. The fungus spreads continuously through wind during the growing season, and spores produced within lesions generate new infection cycles. From the first lesions to severe canopy blighting takes three to six weeks under favorable conditions. The disease progresses at a moderate to fast pace and is cumulative, meaning that without intervention it tends to worsen steadily rather than plateau.

Section: spread
This section is about how the disease spreads.
The primary source of Gray Leaf Spot inoculum is infected corn residue left on the soil surface. This is the defining characteristic that separates it from diseases whose inoculum arrives on wind from distant regions. The fungus survives as dormant structures in residue for one to two seasons and produces conidia during warm, humid conditions that wind carries to lower leaves. No-till and reduced-till fields have dramatically higher risk because residue is retained on the surface rather than buried. Once lesions establish on lower leaves, they produce new conidia that spread upward through the canopy progressively over the season. Long-distance spread by wind does occur across fields and regions during warm, humid weather, but local residue is always the dominant source.

Section: pathogen
This section is about the biology of the pathogen.
Cercospora zeae-maydis is an ascomycete fungus that survives as stromata in corn residue on the soil surface. A closely related species, Cercospora zeina, has been identified particularly in African corn production. The fungus produces long, multiseptate conidia that are dispersed by wind. Infection requires penetration through stomata and depends on extended leaf wetness of at least twelve hours. The fungus produces a photosensitizing toxin called cercosporin that assists tissue colonization by generating reactive oxygen species in the presence of light. Residue on the soil surface can remain infectious for one to two growing seasons, which is why rotation away from corn reduces disease pressure over time.

Section: symptoms
This section is about the symptoms of the disease.
On leaves, Gray Leaf Spot begins as small tan lesions with yellow halos that rapidly expand into rectangular, gray to tan streaks one to six centimeters long. The lesion margins are defined by leaf veins, giving the characteristic rectangular shape that is the primary diagnostic feature of this disease. As the fungus sporulates, lesions develop a velvety gray appearance on the surface. Severe infection causes complete leaf blighting with premature plant death. Sheaths may also develop similar rectangular lesions in severe cases. The disease progresses upward through the canopy from lower to upper leaves, and the most critical timing is when it reaches the ear leaf and above before grain fill begins.

Section: conditions
This section is about the favorable conditions for the disease.
Gray Leaf Spot thrives in temperatures between twenty-two and thirty degrees Celsius with an optimum around twenty-five degrees. It requires at least twelve hours of continuous leaf wetness for infection, which is most commonly provided by prolonged overnight dew in dense canopies. High relative humidity throughout the night is a strong predictor of active infection. The disease is most severe during the period from tasseling through grain fill. No-till fields, dense plant populations, and late-planted corn are consistently the highest-risk situations. Fields in low-lying areas or river bottoms with poor air drainage are particularly vulnerable.

Section: cycle
This section is about the disease cycle.
The cycle begins with the fungus surviving the winter in corn residue on the soil surface. When warm, humid spring conditions arrive, conidia are produced on the residue and wind carries them to the lower leaves of young corn plants. Infection occurs during extended leaf wetness periods. Lesions develop and begin producing new conidia within fourteen to twenty-one days. The disease progresses upward through the canopy through repeated infection cycles driven by wind-dispersed spores and overnight dew. At the end of the season, the fungus returns to residue on the soil surface where it persists until the following season.

Section: treatment
This section is about the treatment of the disease.
The most effective management tool is planting hybrids with high gray leaf spot resistance ratings. Hybrids rated four to seven on a nine-point scale provide meaningful protection, though no completely immune hybrid exists because resistance is polygenic and partial. In no-till fields with a history of severe disease, selecting resistant hybrids is especially important and should be the first management decision. Cultural practices that reduce residue inoculum are highly valuable. Rotating to a non-host crop such as soybeans or small grains for one to two seasons allows residue to break down and substantially reduces the inoculum load. Tillage that buries residue reduces primary inoculum but must be weighed against erosion concerns. Avoiding excessively dense plant populations reduces canopy humidity. Adequate potassium fertility supports plant tolerance. Planting early allows the canopy to senesce before the worst humidity periods of late summer.

Section: management
This section is about the management of the disease.
Fungicide applications are economically justified when disease reaches the ear leaf by tasseling in susceptible hybrids. The optimal timing is at VT to R1, meaning at tasseling through silking. Strobilurins such as azoxystrobin and pyraclostrobin provide good protectant activity. Triazoles such as propiconazole and tebuconazole offer systemic and some curative activity. SDHI products such as fluxapyroxad are excellent and are most effective when used in premix combinations with triazoles or strobilurins. One application at the correct timing is usually sufficient. A second application at R2 may be justified in severe epidemic years on highly susceptible hybrids. Applying after the R4 dough stage has negligible economic return. Fungicide modes of action should be rotated to reduce resistance development. Biological options including Trichoderma-based products and silicon amendments have limited and inconsistent efficacy and are not primary management tools. Crop rotation and resistant varieties remain the most practical and cost-effective approach, especially in no-till production.

Section: actions
This section is about quick actions to take.
Before planting in a high-risk field such as no-till with previous corn, select a resistant hybrid and consider rotating to a non-host crop. From V6 to V10 when lesions appear on lower leaves, begin scouting every five days. When approaching tasseling with lesions on the ear leaf or above, apply fungicide immediately because this is the critical window. After tasseling with severe canopy blighting, apply fungicide if the crop is still between R2 and R3. After harvest in a severely affected field, consider deep tillage or rotation to reduce residue inoculum for the next season.

Section: scouting
This section is about monitoring and scouting.
Begin scouting at V10 and intensify from V14 through tasseling. Sample a minimum of twenty plants per field at five randomly selected locations. Record the percentage of leaf area affected and note the highest leaf position showing lesions. Low-lying and foggy areas of fields often show disease earliest. Scout these areas first. The key threshold for fungicide application is gray leaf spot visible on the ear leaf approaching or at tasseling in a susceptible hybrid.

Section: geography
This section is about the geographic distribution of the disease.
Gray Leaf Spot is a major disease in the Americas, particularly in the US Corn Belt and in Brazil and Argentina. It is also a significant problem in sub-Saharan Africa, especially in highland tropical maize production, and in parts of Asia including India and China. The disease is most destructive in warm, humid highland environments and in no-till production systems wherever corn is grown.

Section: definition
This section is about the definition of the disease.
Early Blight is a fungal disease of potato and tomato caused by Alternaria solani. It is one of the most common and widely distributed foliar diseases of potato worldwide. The disease creates dark spots with distinctive concentric rings that form a bullseye or target pattern, beginning on the oldest lower leaves and progressing steadily upward through the plant. Beyond leaf damage, Early Blight also infects potato tubers, producing sunken corky spots that reduce marketability and increase storage losses. The disease is significantly worsened by plant stress, especially from low nitrogen or drought, and is considered a mid to late season disease that builds as the canopy closes and plants begin to age.

Section: danger
This section is about the severity of the disease.
Early Blight is rated as moderate to high danger depending on crop condition and management. It rarely kills plants directly, but severe defoliation dramatically reduces tuber yield by cutting off photosynthesis before bulking is complete. Yield losses in severe, unmanaged cases range from twenty to forty percent. Tuber quality is also reduced when surface lesions occur, creating entry points for secondary pathogens during storage. The disease is significantly more damaging on stressed crops, particularly those suffering from nitrogen deficiency or drought, making plant nutrition and irrigation management inseparable from disease management.

Section: timeline
This section is about the timeline and spread speed of the disease.
The incubation period is two to three days in warm, humid conditions and extends to up to seven days in cooler weather. The disease builds from mid-season onward, worsening after canopy closure when humidity within the canopy increases. It progresses from the oldest leaves upward over three to six weeks under favorable conditions. The speed of progression is moderate under normal circumstances but accelerates dramatically when plants are under nitrogen or water stress.

Section: spread
This section is about how the disease spreads.
Early Blight overwinters in infected plant debris in the soil and on infected seed tubers. Conidia are dispersed by wind and rain splash from lesions and from debris on the soil surface. The fungus enters host tissue through stomata or by directly penetrating weakened cuticle, especially on older and stressed tissue. Drought stress and low nitrogen dramatically increase susceptibility by weakening plant defenses. Each new lesion produces thousands of fresh conidia within days, providing continuous secondary inoculum throughout the season. Spores can also travel by wind between fields over significant distances.

Section: pathogen
This section is about the biology of the pathogen.
Alternaria solani is an ascomycete fungus that survives in infected plant debris in soil and on infected seed tubers for one to two seasons. It produces large, club-shaped, multicellular conidia with a characteristic long beak, which are readily carried by wind. The fungus also produces toxins including altersolanol and solanopyrone that contribute to tissue necrosis by killing cells ahead of the advancing mycelium. This toxin production explains why lesions expand rapidly even when the fungus itself has not yet colonized the full lesion area. The pathogen infects both potato and tomato and can move between the two crops, making proximity of these crops a risk factor.

Section: symptoms
This section is about the symptoms of the disease.
On leaves, Early Blight produces dark brown to black lesions with distinct concentric rings that create the classic bullseye or target pattern. A yellow chlorotic halo surrounds each lesion. Lesions range from three to fifteen millimeters in diameter. The disease begins exclusively on older, lower leaves and progresses upward over the season. Severely infected leaves turn yellow and drop, reducing the photosynthetic capacity of the plant. On stems, dark elongated sunken lesions can appear, occasionally girdling young stems and causing shoot death. Petiole lesions may cause premature leaf drop before the leaf itself is fully killed. On tubers, Early Blight produces dark, sunken, circular to irregular corky lesions on the surface that are five to fifteen millimeters in diameter with slightly raised margins. Below the skin, a shallow, dry, corky rot is visible. These lesions reduce marketability significantly and serve as entry points for secondary pathogens during storage.

Section: conditions
This section is about the favorable conditions for the disease.
Early Blight thrives in temperatures between twenty-four and twenty-nine degrees Celsius with an optimum around twenty-seven degrees. High humidity and periods of leaf wetness are required for infection. The disease is most damaging on senescent, stressed, or nitrogen-deficient plants.
