It builds particularly after canopy closure when humidity within the crop increases. Dense plantings and mid to late season timing are consistent risk factors.

Section: cycle
This section is about the disease cycle.
The cycle begins with the fungus overwintering in infected plant debris in the soil and on infected seed tubers. When warm, humid conditions arrive in the growing season, conidia are released from debris and dispersed by wind and rain splash. Infection occurs through stomata or directly through weakened cuticle on older tissue. After an incubation of two to seven days, new lesions form and immediately begin producing fresh conidia. Multiple cycles repeat throughout the season. At the end of the season, infected debris falls to the soil and perpetuates the cycle, while infected tubers can also carry the pathogen into the next season and into storage.

Section: treatment
This section is about the treatment of the disease.
Plant nutrition management is the most critical non-chemical tool for Early Blight. Maintaining adequate nitrogen throughout the season is essential because nitrogen-deficient plants develop far more severe disease under the same inoculum levels as well-fertilized plants. Potassium adequacy also supports disease tolerance. Consistent irrigation to prevent drought stress is equally important, and drip irrigation is preferred over overhead systems to keep foliage dry. Crop rotation away from potato, tomato, and other solanaceous hosts for two to three seasons reduces debris inoculum in the soil. Certified disease-free seed tubers should always be used. Removing infected plant debris after harvest and early haulm destruction before harvest reduce tuber infection pressure.

Section: management
This section is about the management of the disease.
Fungicide applications should begin when disease first appears or when conditions consistently favor infection, which means warm temperatures combined with humid nights. Applications every seven to ten days are typical, increasing to every five to seven days during wet, warm periods. Multi-site protectants such as chlorothalonil and mancozeb are applied preventively. Systemic fungicides including strobilurins such as azoxystrobin and pyraclostrobin, triazoles such as difenoconazole and tebuconazole, and SDHI products provide both preventive and curative activity and are valuable when disease is already present. Modes of action must be rotated with every two applications because strobilurin resistance in Alternaria populations is increasingly documented. Thorough coverage of lower canopy leaves is important because this is where the disease initiates. In organic programs, copper-based fungicides are the most reliable option. Bacillus subtilis products have some suppressive activity and can support an integrated program. Stress management through nutrition and irrigation remains the most impactful organic intervention.

Section: actions
This section is about quick actions to take.
When bullseye spots appear on lower leaves, apply fungicide and check nitrogen levels immediately. When plants show drought stress alongside disease, irrigate first and then apply fungicide after irrigation. Before hot weather above twenty-four degrees combined with humid nights, apply a protectant fungicide in advance. When disease is spreading rapidly up the canopy, switch to a systemic fungicide and address all stress factors simultaneously. At harvest, when tubers show corky spots, assess and improve the rotation plan and storage conditions for the following season.

Section: scouting
This section is about monitoring and scouting.
Begin scouting at canopy closure and intensify mid-season. Sample twenty to thirty plants per field from multiple locations. Record the percentage of plants with symptoms, the highest leaf position affected, and the number of lesions per leaf. Check the oldest leaves at the base of the plant first because disease always begins there. If leaf color is pale or yellow alongside Early Blight symptoms, nitrogen correction is urgent. Track weather periods of three or more consecutive days with temperatures above twenty-four degrees combined with rain or heavy dew, as these represent high-risk windows.

Section: geography
This section is about the geographic distribution of the disease.
Early Blight is present globally wherever potato and tomato are grown. It is most severe in warm, humid continental and subtropical climates. In temperate regions it is a mid to late season disease. In tropical production regions it operates as a year-round problem. It is consistently among the most common foliar diseases reported by potato growers worldwide.

Section: definition
This section is about the definition of the disease.
Early Blight is a fungal disease caused by Alternaria solani that attacks tomato plants throughout the growing season, particularly in warm, wet conditions. Despite its name, it can occur at any stage of plant development and is one of the most common and widespread foliar diseases of tomato worldwide. The disease causes distinctive target-like lesions on leaves, stems, and fruit that lead to progressive defoliation from the bottom of the plant upward. It is present in all tomato-producing regions and is favored by the warm temperatures and alternating wet and dry periods common in many tomato-growing climates.

Section: danger
This section is about the severity of the disease.
Early Blight is rated as a moderate to high danger disease. While it rarely causes total crop failure in a single season, it can cause severe defoliation that reduces fruit quality through sunscald, decreases yield through reduced photosynthesis, and weakens plants making them more susceptible to other stresses. The progressive nature of the disease, beginning at the base of the plant and moving upward, means that if not managed it will eventually reach the upper canopy and affect fruit-bearing tissue. Economic losses of twenty to thirty percent are common in unmanaged susceptible varieties during wet seasons.

Section: timeline
This section is about the timeline and spread speed of the disease.
The incubation period is three to five days under warm, humid conditions, and spore production begins rapidly after lesion formation. The disease is capable of multiple infection cycles per week under ideal conditions. Early in the season, spread is primarily from soil-splashed conidia infecting lower leaves. As the season progresses and the canopy fills, upper leaf spread via windborne conidia becomes more important. The disease progresses most rapidly during periods of warm temperatures combined with alternating wet and dry weather.

Section: spread
This section is about how the disease spreads.
Alternaria solani overwinters in infected plant debris and in the soil, where it can persist for extended periods. Conidia produced from infected debris are rain-splashed onto lower leaves, initiating primary infections early in the season. From these initial lesions, new conidia are produced and spread by wind and rain splash throughout the canopy. The pathogen can also be seedborne, allowing introduction via infected transplants or direct-seeded crops. Stressed plants, particularly those experiencing drought stress, nutrient deficiency, or heavy fruit loads, are significantly more susceptible than well-managed plants.

Section: pathogen
This section is about the biology of the pathogen.
Alternaria solani is a deuteromycete fungus that produces large, distinctive club-shaped conidia with a long beak. It is a necrotrophic pathogen that kills host tissue in advance of colonization using cell wall-degrading enzymes and toxins. The fungus can survive for extended periods in soil and plant debris as dormant mycelium and spores. It has a wide host range within the Solanaceae family, also infecting potato, eggplant, and pepper. Multiple pathogen strains with varying levels of fungicide resistance exist in commercial production areas.

Section: symptoms
This section is about the symptoms of the disease.
On leaves, Early Blight causes the disease's characteristic target-like lesions: dark brown, irregularly circular spots two to four centimeters in diameter with distinct concentric rings forming a bull's-eye pattern. A yellow halo surrounds mature lesions. Lesions first appear on older, lower leaves and progress upward through the canopy. Severely infected leaves turn yellow and drop, exposing fruit. On stems, the fungus causes brown, slightly sunken lesions at the soil line or on stem tissue, sometimes called collar rot when it infects the stem base of young transplants. On fruit, dark, sunken lesions develop near the stem end that have the same target-like concentric ring pattern as leaf lesions. Fruit lesions may become large and are often colonized by secondary pathogens.

Section: conditions
This section is about the favorable conditions for the disease.
Early Blight thrives in temperatures between twenty-four and thirty degrees Celsius but can cause infections across a wide range from fifteen to thirty-five degrees. The fungus requires free water on leaf surfaces for spore germination and infection. Alternating wet and dry periods are particularly favorable, as spores are produced during dry periods and dispersed effectively, then germinate when wet conditions return. Plant stress from drought, heavy fruit loads, or nutrient deficiencies significantly increases susceptibility.

Section: cycle
This section is about the disease cycle.
The cycle begins with the fungus overwintering in infected plant debris or soil. Conidia from debris infect lower leaves in early season via rain splash. Infected leaves produce abundant conidia that spread upward through the canopy during warm weather. The disease progresses from the bottom of the plant upward throughout the growing season. At the end of the season, infected tissue returns to the soil surface and serves as inoculum for the following season.

Section: treatment
This section is about the treatment of the disease.
Cultural management focuses on crop rotation with non-solanaceous crops for at least two years to reduce soilborne inoculum, removing and destroying infected plant debris after harvest, and using certified pathogen-free transplants. Mulching the soil surface reduces rain splash of conidia from soil and debris onto lower leaves. Stake and cage systems that keep foliage off the soil also reduce splash infection of lower leaves. Maintaining plant health through adequate irrigation and balanced nutrition reduces stress susceptibility. Variety selection offers some assistance. While no commercial variety is fully resistant, some varieties show moderate resistance that reduces disease severity compared to highly susceptible varieties.

Section: management
This section is about the management of the disease.
Chemical control should begin when the first lesions appear or when weather conditions become favorable, whichever comes first. Fungicide classes used include multi-site protectants such as chlorothalonil, mancozeb, and copper; and single-site fungicides including strobilurins such as azoxystrobin and pyraclostrobin, and SDHI products. Applications should be made on seven to ten day intervals during favorable weather. Strobilurins should be rotated with other modes of action to manage resistance, as strobilurin-resistant Alternaria solani populations are documented in some production areas. Systemic fungicides provide both protective and limited curative activity within one to two days of infection.

Section: actions
This section is about quick actions to take.
At transplanting, apply a protective fungicide to lower leaves and mulch the soil surface. When first lesions appear on lower leaves, immediately remove and destroy heavily infected lower leaves and apply a systemic fungicide. During warm, wet periods, maintain seven-day spray intervals without gaps. When defoliation reaches the middle canopy, assess the economic justification for continued management. At the end of the season, remove and destroy all infected plant tissue to reduce inoculum for the following season.

Section: scouting
This section is about monitoring and scouting.
Scout fields weekly from transplanting through harvest. Begin inspection of the lowest leaves on the oldest plants in the field, as this is where the disease consistently appears first. Assess the percentage of leaves with lesions and the height to which the disease has progressed in the canopy relative to fruit-bearing tissue. Monitor plant stress indicators such as wilting, yellowing, or heavy fruit loads that increase susceptibility.

Section: geography
This section is about the geographic distribution of the disease.
Early Blight of tomato is present in all major tomato-producing regions worldwide. It is most severe in regions with warm temperatures and alternating wet and dry weather, including the eastern United States, parts of Europe, and many tropical and subtropical tomato-growing regions. It is a year-round concern in tropical climates where tomatoes are grown continuously.

Section: definition
This section is about the definition of the disease.
Esca is a complex and incurable wood disease of grapevines caused by a consortium of fungi that slowly colonize and rot the internal woody tissue of the trunk and arms. It has been recognized in vineyards for over two thousand years and remains one of the most serious threats to mature vines worldwide. On the outside, the disease produces a striking tiger-stripe pattern on leaves and dark spots on berries known as black measles. Internally, the wood decays progressively until the vine's vascular system can no longer function. In its most severe acute form, called apoplexy, a vine can collapse and die within two to five days during a summer heat wave. Because the wood cannot be disinfected once infected, Esca is defined by prevention rather than cure.

Section: danger
This section is about the severity of the disease.
Esca is rated as very high danger and is unique in being both chronic and incurable. It can kill grapevines through two distinct pathways. The chronic form causes gradual decline over years as internal wood decay reduces the vine's ability to transport water and nutrients. The acute form, apoplexy, causes sudden total collapse of the vine within days when heat stress pushes a severely infected vine past its physiological limit. Yield loss is significant every year in symptomatic vines, and apoplexy cases result in one hundred percent loss for the affected vine. The global economic cost of Esca is estimated in the billions of dollars annually. Mature vineyards older than fifteen years typically show incidence rates of five to thirty percent, which can reach fifty percent or more in severely affected blocks.

Section: timeline
This section is about the timeline and spread speed of the disease.
Within an individual vine, the fungi spread slowly through woody tissue over months to years. External symptoms may not appear for three to ten years after initial infection, meaning a vine can be diseased long before any visible sign is present. Between vines, spores spread at pruning time through wind and contaminated tools. The chronic disease form is very slow, developing over many seasons. The apoplectic form, in contrast, is extremely sudden once triggered by heat stress, with complete vine collapse occurring within two to five days.

Section: spread
This section is about how the disease spreads.
Esca spreads primarily through fresh pruning wounds. When cuts are made in the vine, the exposed wood surface is colonized by airborne spores within hours. These spores are produced on infected wood in the vineyard and travel on wind to land on freshly cut surfaces. Contaminated pruning tools are an equally important vector. When an infected vine is cut and the same unsterilized tool is used on a healthy vine, the fungi are transferred directly in the sap on the blade. Infected nursery stock is a major source of new vineyard infections, as vines can carry the pathogen internally without showing any symptoms at planting. Esca does not spread through soil, through root contact, or through irrigation water. Once inside the vine, the fungi grow progressively through the woody tissue toward the roots over many seasons.

Section: pathogen
This section is about the biology of the pathogen.
Esca is caused by three main fungal species acting together as a complex. Phaeomoniella chlamydospora is an early colonizer that causes brown and black staining of the vascular tissue. Phaeoacremonium minimum, formerly known as P. aleophilum, is a vascular pathogen that causes the characteristic esca proper. Fomitiporia mediterranea is a basidiomycete that causes white spongy rot in the older central wood. These fungi produce spores under wet conditions that are released and carried by wind to pruning wounds. They survive for extended periods inside woody tissue and in infected pruning stubs. Their slow growth through wood explains the long latency period between infection and symptom expression.

Section: symptoms
This section is about the symptoms of the disease. On leaves, Esca produces interveinal chlorosis with reddish-brown necrotic spots that create the distinctive tiger stripe pattern. Leaf margins may dry, brown, and curl inward. These symptoms typically appear suddenly in mid-summer and may vary year to year, with symptomatic vines sometimes appearing healthy the following season. On berries, small dark purple to black spots appear on the berry surface with lighter halos. These spots are the black measles for which the disease is named. Affected berries crack and shrivel at the spots, making entire clusters unmarketable. In the wood, cutting across the trunk reveals the most reliable diagnostic signs. Brown or black staining in the vascular tissue and white, soft, spongy rot in the older central wood confirm Esca. The infected wood has a characteristic dirty or decayed texture and may show gummosis at pruning wounds. Apoplexy presents as sudden wilting and death of all shoots on one or more arms, or the entire vine, within two to five days. The foliage collapses as if from severe drought but does not recover when irrigated.

Section: symptoms
Cutting the trunk confirms infection through the dark internal staining.

Section: conditions
This section is about the favorable conditions for the disease.
Esca is primarily a disease of mature vines. Vines under five years old are rarely affected. Older vines become progressively more susceptible as their woody structure accumulates pruning wounds and internal decay. Infection occurs at pruning time, particularly when wounds are exposed to rain or wet conditions shortly after cutting. Heat waves in summer trigger apoplexy in vines that already carry substantial internal fungal load. Drought, heat, and nutrient deficiency worsen the probability and severity of apoplexy. The disease is present worldwide but is worst in warm, Mediterranean, and semi-arid climates.

Section: cycle
This section is about the disease cycle.
The cycle begins when airborne spores from infected wood or nursery stock land on fresh pruning wounds. The fungi colonize the wound surface and grow into the woody tissue progressively over months and years. The internal wood decays slowly while the vine continues to function. Eventually the fungal load disrupts vascular function enough to produce external symptoms. In summers with extreme heat, the infected vascular system fails catastrophically, causing apoplexy. Infected pruning stubs in the vineyard continuously produce spores during wet weather throughout the year, infecting adjacent vines season after season.

Section: treatment
This section is about the treatment of the disease. Because there is no cure once the wood is infected, all management is preventive. The most important practice is protecting pruning wounds immediately after every cut. Wound protectants including thiophanate-methyl fungicide paint and formulations containing Trichoderma harzianum should be applied to every cut surface within hours of pruning. These products colonize the wound and exclude the Esca pathogens before they can establish. Pruning tools must be sterilized between every vine using ten percent bleach solution or seventy percent alcohol. Carrying two pairs of shears and alternating between them while the other soaks in sterilant is a practical method. Pruning during dry weather reduces spore release. Pruning during the coldest part of winter, when fungal activity is lowest, reduces infection risk. Double pruning, which involves a rough cut in autumn followed by the final cut in late winter, minimizes the time wounds are exposed during high-risk periods. New vineyards should be established only with certified disease-free planting material.

Section: treatment
Every vine should be inspected at the graft union and a cross-section of the wood should be examined for dark staining before accepting any nursery material. When infection is discovered early, surgical interventions can extend vine life. Trunk renewal removes the infected trunk entirely and trains a new shoot from the rootstock base. This is effective when disease has not yet reached the roots and typically requires three to five years to restore full production. Curettage involves surgically carving out infected wood from the trunk and sealing the wound with a biological protectant. Removing individual infected arms while the trunk remains is feasible for early or localized infections.

Section: management
This section is about the management of the disease.
No systemic fungicide eliminates established infections inside the wood. Chemical treatments are effective only as wound protectants applied immediately at the time of pruning.

Section: actions
This section is about quick actions to take.
When about to prune, prepare sterilization solution before starting and apply wound protectant to every cut immediately after making it. When tiger-stripe leaf symptoms appear mid-summer, mark affected vines with flagging tape, assess the number of affected vines, and plan trunk renewal for vines that have shown symptoms for two or more consecutive years. When a vine collapses suddenly, cut the trunk to confirm internal staining, remove the vine, and evaluate the surrounding vines for early symptoms. When planting a new vineyard, inspect every vine's wood cross-section before accepting the material and refuse any showing dark staining.

Section: scouting
This section is about monitoring and scouting.
Survey the entire vineyard annually in mid-summer, which is July or August in the northern hemisphere, when foliar symptoms are most visible. Record all symptomatic vines by location using GPS or a vineyard map. Track the progression of each marked vine year over year to identify those worsening consistently. During dormant pruning, cross-section flagged vines to confirm internal staining and assess how deep the decay has progressed. Track apoplexy events during summer heat waves as a measure of the disease's severity in the block.

Section: geography
This section is about the geographic distribution of the disease.
Esca is present in all grape-growing regions worldwide. It is most economically damaging in France, Italy, Spain, Portugal, California, and Australia. Incidence in mature vineyards typically ranges from five to thirty percent and can exceed fifty percent in the most affected blocks. The disease became significantly more prevalent in European vineyards after sodium arsenite, a highly effective wood protectant, was banned in 2002.

Section: definition
This section is about the definition of the disease.
Grape Downy Mildew is one of the most destructive diseases in viticulture worldwide, caused by Plasmopara viticola, an oomycete or water mold rather than a true fungus. This distinction is critical because it means standard fungicides effective against true fungi do not work against this pathogen, and only products specifically labeled for oomycete diseases can provide control. The disease creates pale yellow oil spots on the upper surface of leaves and a characteristic white, downy growth on the underside. It attacks leaves, shoots, tendrils, and fruit clusters with devastating speed during warm, rainy weather. A single warm, wet night can initiate a massive infection event across an entire vineyard. European wine grape varieties are extremely susceptible, while American varieties carry natural partial resistance. Plasmopara viticola originated in North America and was accidentally introduced to Europe in the 1870s, nearly destroying the European wine industry before control measures were developed.

Section: danger
This section is about the severity of the disease.
Grape Downy Mildew is rated as very high danger. Repeated severe defoliation weakens vines significantly, and young vines can be killed outright in their first season. Yield losses in unprotected susceptible varieties during wet seasons commonly reach fifty to one hundred percent. The economic impact is catastrophic when cluster infection occurs, because infected grapes are unmarketable and produce off-flavors in wine. The disease spreads so rapidly that by the time oil spots are visible, the vineyard may already be heavily infected beyond what can be seen. Once established, systemic fungicides provide only limited curative activity of twenty-four to seventy-two hours, making preventive protection essential.

Section: timeline
This section is about the timeline and spread speed of the disease.
The incubation period ranges from five to eighteen days, with shorter periods in warm weather around twenty degrees Celsius and longer periods in cool conditions. During a rain event, sporangia released from sporulating lesions disperse to the entire vineyard within hours through wind and rain. From the first oil spots to complete canopy infection takes only one to three weeks in warm, wet conditions. This makes Downy Mildew one of the fastest-spreading diseases in all of viticulture.

Section: spread
This section is about how the disease spreads.
The primary overwintering source is oospores, which are sexually produced thick-walled spores that survive in fallen infected leaves and in the soil. In spring, oospores germinate after rain and release zoospores that swim through water films on leaf surfaces to stomata, where they initiate primary infections. The trigger point for primary infection risk is captured in the ten-ten-ten rule: the disease begins when shoots reach ten centimeters, air temperature exceeds ten degrees Celsius, and ten millimeters of rain have fallen in the previous twenty-four hours. Once primary infections establish, the white downy growth on leaf undersides produces sporangia that contain more zoospores. Rain and wind disperse these sporangia across the entire vineyard. Cluster infection is most devastating from flowering through berry set, when infected flowers and young berries turn brown and die. Long-distance introduction of the pathogen occurs through infected planting material brought to new vineyards.

Section: pathogen
This section is about the biology of the pathogen.
Plasmopara viticola is an oomycete, a group of organisms more closely related to algae and brown algae than to true fungi. This evolutionary distance explains why fungicides that work by targeting fungal-specific biochemical pathways are ineffective against it. The pathogen produces two types of asexual spores: macrosporangia that are dispersed by wind and rain, and zoospores released from the macrosporangia in the presence of water that swim through water films to reach stomata. The sexual stage produces oospores through the mating of compatible strains, and these oospores provide the long-term survival in soil across seasons. The pathogen has shown the ability to evolve resistance to phenylamide fungicides such as metalaxyl, and this resistance is now widespread globally.

Section: symptoms
This section is about the symptoms of the disease. On the upper leaf surface, Downy Mildew produces pale yellow to lime-green oil spots that appear translucent and greasy when held to light. These spots are roughly circular but are sometimes limited by leaf veins into angular shapes. Multiple spots coalesce to cover large areas of the leaf, which eventually turn brown and die. On the lower leaf surface, directly beneath the oil spots, a white, cottony, fluffy growth appears. This is the sporangial growth, the production of infectious spore packets, and it is the primary diagnostic sign of active Downy Mildew. The color of this growth can range from pure white in early season infections to grayish or brownish as oospores form later in the season. On shoots and tendrils, brown to black lesions form and shoot tips may die and curl into a shepherd's crook shape. Infected tendrils fail to grip and lose their structural function. On clusters, infected flower clusters turn brown and die before they can set fruit, which is the most devastating outcome. Young infected berries turn gray-brown in a condition called grey rot or leather rot, then harden and shrivel.

Section: symptoms
Late-season berry infections can show white sporulation under humid conditions.

Section: conditions
This section is about the favorable conditions for the disease.
Downy Mildew develops in temperatures between twelve and thirty degrees Celsius with an optimum between twenty and twenty-five degrees. Rain is required for oospore germination, for sporangia dispersal, and for zoospore release and movement to stomata. Overnight dew or rain provides the leaf wetness that enables infection. The disease is most damaging from flowering through veraison, the point of color change in berries. European wine grape varieties including Chardonnay, Merlot, Cabernet Sauvignon, and Riesling have essentially no meaningful resistance and are fully susceptible.

Section: cycle
This section is about the disease cycle.
Oospores overwinter in soil and infected leaf litter. Spring rains trigger oospore germination, producing macrosporangia that release zoospores capable of infecting leaves through stomata. Primary infection begins when the ten-ten-ten rule conditions are met. After an incubation of five to eighteen days, oil spots appear and white sporangial growth develops on the leaf underside. Rain and wind disperse new sporangia across the vineyard. Multiple infection cycles occur throughout the season during warm, wet weather. At season end, sexual reproduction between compatible strains produces oospores within infected tissue, which fall with leaves to the soil and survive until the following spring.

Section: treatment
This section is about the treatment of the disease.
Canopy management is the most important cultural practice. Leaf removal and shoot positioning improve air circulation and reduce humidity around clusters, significantly lowering infection risk. Removing leaves around clusters at bloom directly reduces the risk of the most economically damaging cluster infection. Overhead irrigation should be avoided. Leaf litter should be shredded and incorporated at season end to reduce oospore survival. Rows should be oriented to maximize sunlight and airflow. Variety choice is the most powerful long-term tool. American species and PIWI varieties, which are European-American hybrids bred for disease resistance, carry significant natural resistance. PIWI varieties such as Regent, Johanniter, Bronner, Solaris, Cabernet Blanc, and Soreli can reduce fungicide requirements by fifty to eighty percent compared to susceptible European varieties. Pure Vitis vinifera varieties have no meaningful resistance and require full chemical protection programs.

Section: management
This section is about the management of the disease. Chemical control must use only products specifically labeled for downy mildew and active against oomycetes, because products designed for true fungal diseases will not work. Protectant fungicides including mancozeb, folpet, and copper coat the leaf surface and prevent infection but provide no curative activity. They must be reapplied after significant rain. Systemic fungicides provide penetration into leaf tissue and some curative activity. Phenylamide products such as metalaxyl and mefenoxam have systemic action but resistance is widespread and they must never be used alone, always in premix with a protectant. Carboxylic acid amide products such as dimethomorph, iprovalicarb, and mandipropamid are systemic with excellent anti-sporulation activity and some curative action up to forty-eight hours after infection. Cymoxanil provides curative activity up to twenty-four hours post-infection. The spray program should begin when shoots reach ten centimeters or when the ten-ten-ten trigger is met, whichever comes first. Intervals are seven to ten days in wet weather and fourteen days in dry periods.

Section: management
Systemic fungicides should be prioritized at bloom for maximum protection of flowers. Metalaxyl and mefenoxam should be rotated with CAA products and cymoxanil-containing products, and should always be used only in premix formulations to manage resistance risk. In organic viticulture, copper-based fungicides are the primary tool and can be effective protectants when applied on schedule. Potassium phosphite triggers plant defense responses and provides limited but real suppression and is approved in some organic programs. Bacillus subtilis has limited suppressive activity and plays a supportive role. Canopy management and PIWI variety selection are the most impactful organic strategies.

Section: actions
This section is about quick actions to take.
When shoots reach ten centimeters in a wet spring, begin the protectant fungicide program immediately without waiting for symptoms. When the ten-ten-ten rule is triggered, apply a systemic plus protectant tank mix before the next rain event. When oil spots become visible on leaves, apply a systemic fungicide immediately and increase spray frequency to every five to seven days. At the beginning of bloom, apply a systemic fungicide regardless of weather because this is the single most critical spray timing to protect flowers. When white sporulation is visible on lesions, indicating an active epidemic, spray every five to seven days and remove infected shoots.

Section: scouting
This section is about monitoring and scouting.
Monitor shoots daily once they reach five centimeters because the ten-ten-ten trigger can arrive suddenly. Check both leaf surfaces at every scouting visit, looking for oil spots on the upper surface and white sporulation on the lower surface. Pay special attention to the youngest shoots and cluster zones. Track sporulation intensity because sporulating lesions indicate active epidemic spread and require an immediate response. After any rain event meeting the ten-ten-ten conditions, scout for oil spots five to ten days later. Regional disease forecasting systems can help optimize spray timing where available.

Section: geography
This section is about the geographic distribution of the disease.
Downy Mildew originated in North America and was introduced to Europe in 1878 with near-catastrophic consequences for the wine industry. Today it is present in all grape-growing regions worldwide. It is most severe in humid continental and maritime climates such as France, Germany, Italy, the eastern United States, and China. In dry Mediterranean climates it causes less pressure but can still produce severe losses in wet years. It is also damaging in South America, South Africa, and Australia.

Section: definition
This section is about the definition of the disease.
Huanglongbing, also known as Citrus Greening or HLB, is a bacterial disease caused by Candidatus Liberibacter asiaticus that is widely regarded as the most destructive citrus disease in the world. It is spread by the Asian citrus psyllid insect and has no known cure. Once a tree is infected, it is infected for life and will eventually decline and die. The disease disrupts the phloem tissue of the tree, preventing the normal transport of nutrients and carbohydrates. Infected trees produce small, lopsided, bitter, and commercially worthless fruit. The disease has devastated commercial citrus production in Florida, parts of Asia, and threatens all citrus-growing regions where the psyllid vector is present.

Section: danger
This section is about the severity of the disease.
Huanglongbing is rated as the highest danger citrus disease known. It is incurable, systemic, and fatal to every commercial citrus variety. There is no known resistant citrus variety. A single infected tree can serve as a source from which the psyllid vector spreads the disease to surrounding trees. In Florida, HLB has reduced citrus production by over eighty percent since its detection in 2005. The disease kills infected trees within five to ten years of infection under commercial production conditions, though the timeline can be shortened by additional stress factors. The economic impact is catastrophic wherever the disease establishes.

Section: timeline
This section is about the timeline and spread speed of the disease.
The incubation period after infection by the psyllid vector ranges from several months to over one year before symptoms become visible. This long latent period is one of the most dangerous aspects of HLB, as infected trees appear healthy while serving as inoculum sources for psyllid-mediated spread. Once symptoms appear, tree decline accelerates over one to several years. The disease spreads through a grove at a rate determined by psyllid population density and the proportion of infected trees serving as inoculum sources.

Section: spread
This section is about how the disease spreads.
Huanglongbing is spread almost exclusively by the Asian citrus psyllid, Diaphorina citri. Psyllids acquire the bacterium by feeding on phloem tissue of an infected tree and can then transmit it to healthy trees during subsequent feeding. The bacterium is not seed-transmitted under most conditions and does not spread through soil, water, or air contact between trees. Infected budwood used for grafting spreads the disease over long distances and was responsible for much of the initial global spread. Psyllid populations must be managed to slow the spread of the disease within and between groves.

Section: pathogen
This section is about the biology of the pathogen.
Candidatus Liberibacter asiaticus is a gram-negative, phloem-limited bacterium that cannot be cultured outside a living host. It colonizes phloem sieve tubes throughout the tree, disrupting carbohydrate and nutrient transport. The bacterium is transmitted in a persistent, circulative manner by the Asian citrus psyllid, meaning that the psyllid acquires the bacterium, it multiplies within the psyllid, and the psyllid remains infectious for the rest of its life. The bacterium is also present in the salivary glands of infected psyllids and is inoculated into phloem tissue during feeding.

Section: symptoms
This section is about the symptoms of the disease.
On leaves, Huanglongbing causes a distinctive asymmetric or blotchy mottling pattern called blotchy mottle that does not correspond to the vein pattern of the leaf. This pattern distinguishes HLB from nutritional deficiencies, which typically produce symmetric patterns. Leaves on affected branches may show yellow shoot, where an entire branch or flush of growth turns yellow. On fruit, infected trees produce small, lopsided fruit that remain partially green at the stylar end even when mature, giving the disease the name Citrus Greening. The fruit is bitter, poorly flavored, and commercially worthless. Seeds within infected fruit are often brown and aborted. On the whole tree, advanced HLB causes general decline with sparse canopy, dieback of branches, small undersized fruit, and eventual death of the tree over several years.

Section: conditions
This section is about the favorable conditions for the disease.
Huanglongbing can infect citrus under all climate conditions where the Asian citrus psyllid survives. Psyllid populations are highest during warm weather and during periods of new vegetative flush, which is the primary feeding and egg-laying site for the insect. High psyllid populations combined with a high proportion of infected source trees accelerate disease spread within a grove. The bacterium has no particular temperature or humidity requirements beyond those needed for the host tree and vector to survive.

Section: cycle
This section is about the disease cycle.
The cycle begins with psyllids feeding on infected citrus tissue and acquiring the bacterium. After a latent period within the psyllid, the insect becomes infectious and transmits the bacterium to healthy trees during phloem feeding. The bacterium multiplies within the new host and spreads systemically through the phloem over months to years. As more trees become infected, they serve as inoculum sources for psyllids that continue to spread the disease. There is no recovery or cure once infection is established.

Section: treatment
This section is about the treatment of the disease.
There is no cure for Huanglongbing. Management focuses on slowing the spread of the disease by reducing psyllid populations and removing infected trees that serve as inoculum sources. Aggressive psyllid control through insecticide applications is the primary tool available to commercial growers. Infected trees should be removed and destroyed as quickly as possible after confirmation to reduce inoculum pressure. Thermotherapy, trunk injection of antibiotics such as oxytetracycline, and nutritional management programs have shown some ability to temporarily reduce symptom severity and improve fruit production in infected trees, but none of these treatments eliminate the pathogen or restore tree health to pre-infection levels. Research into resistant rootstocks and varieties is ongoing but has not yet produced commercially viable solutions.

Section: management
This section is about the management of the disease.
Integrated management programs combine intensive psyllid monitoring and control, rapid removal of symptomatic trees, use of certified pathogen-free nursery stock, and nutritional support programs for infected trees that growers choose to maintain in production. Psyllid control relies on systemic insecticides applied to soil or foliage, with soil drenches providing longer residual activity. The goal of psyllid control is to reduce vector populations below thresholds that allow rapid disease spread. Entire region-wide coordinated management programs have been established in Florida and other affected areas to address the landscape-scale nature of the disease.

Section: actions
This section is about quick actions to take.
If yellow shoot symptoms or blotchy mottle is observed, submit samples for laboratory confirmation immediately. While awaiting results, apply a systemic insecticide to control psyllids on affected and adjacent trees. Upon laboratory confirmation of HLB, notify local agricultural authorities as required. Remove and destroy confirmed infected trees as quickly as possible to reduce inoculum. Increase psyllid monitoring frequency on remaining trees and apply insecticides on a regular schedule.

Section: scouting
This section is about monitoring and scouting.
Scout trees monthly for blotchy mottle symptoms on leaves and yellowing shoots. During periods of vegetative flush, inspect new growth for psyllid eggs and nymphs. Submit suspect samples to a certified laboratory for polymerase chain reaction testing to confirm HLB, as visual diagnosis alone is insufficient. Track psyllid populations using sticky traps and direct inspection. Participate in area-wide monitoring programs where available.

Section: geography
This section is about the geographic distribution of the disease.
Huanglongbing is present in virtually all citrus-producing regions of Asia, where it originated. It is established in Florida in the United States, Brazil, and parts of Africa. It has been detected in California and Texas and is considered a severe threat to all citrus-producing regions of the Americas and the Mediterranean where the Asian citrus psyllid is present or could become established.

Section: definition
This section is about the definition of the disease.
Potato Late Blight is the most dangerous potato disease in the world, caused by Phytophthora infestans, an oomycete or water mold rather than a true fungus. It is historically responsible for the Irish Famine of 1845 to 1852, during which it destroyed the potato crop across Ireland causing over one million deaths and the emigration of two million more. Today it remains an active and serious threat, costing global agriculture more than six billion dollars annually. The disease creates large, dark, water-soaked spots on leaves with white cottony mold underneath, and causes reddish-brown rot inside tubers that can spread through an entire storage facility. It spreads with extreme speed, capable of destroying an entire field in one to two weeks and traveling by wind for kilometers to infect neighboring farms. Because it is an oomycete and not a true fungus, most standard fungicides have no effect on it, and only oomycete-active products can provide control.

Section: danger
This section is about the severity of the disease.
Late Blight is rated as critical, the highest possible danger level for any plant disease. It can kill entire plants and entire fields within seven to fourteen days under favorable conditions. Yield losses can reach one hundred percent in the field, with additional storage losses when infected tubers are mixed with healthy ones. The speed of destruction is extreme, making it unlike any other common crop disease. Any confirmed sighting in the field should be treated as an immediate emergency.

Section: timeline
This section is about the timeline and spread speed of the disease.
The incubation period is just two to four days under cool, wet conditions, one of the shortest of any major crop disease. An entire field can collapse within seven to fourteen days of first infection under favorable weather. Spores travel by wind for kilometers, meaning a single infected field can endanger every potato farm in the surrounding region. Infected tubers spread disease to healthy tubers in storage, and an entire storage bin can be lost in weeks from a single infected lot.

Section: spread
This section is about how the disease spreads.
The primary overwintering source is infected potato tubers, whether left in the field as volunteers, stored in facilities, or discarded in cull piles near the field. These infected tubers begin sporulating under cool, wet spring conditions and release spores that are carried by wind. Rain releases and disperses sporangia from infected plant material. Zoospores released from sporangia in the presence of water infect tubers through lenticels and wounds when sporangia are washed into the soil by rain. In storage, infected tubers produce mycelium that spreads to adjacent healthy tubers, and secondary bacterial soft rots rapidly accelerate the destruction. Cull piles of discarded infected potatoes left near fields are a major and often overlooked inoculum source that should always be destroyed immediately.

Section: pathogen
This section is about the biology of the pathogen.
Phytophthora infestans is an oomycete, biologically more similar to algae and diatoms than to true fungi. This evolutionary distance is why fungicides targeting fungal-specific processes such as ergosterol biosynthesis have no activity against it. Only oomycete-specific products work. The pathogen produces sporangia that can either germinate directly at cool temperatures or release biflagellate zoospores at warmer temperatures, giving it flexibility across a range of conditions. Where both A1 and A2 mating types are present, sexual reproduction produces oospores that survive in soil for multiple years, complicating long-term management. Metalaxyl and mefenoxam resistance is extremely common worldwide and must be assumed unless specifically proven otherwise in a given region. The pathogen continues to evolve, and new genetic lineages emerge regularly that can overcome host resistance genes.

Section: symptoms
This section is about the symptoms of the disease.
On leaves, Late Blight produces large, irregular, water-soaked lesions that begin pale green and rapidly transition to dark brown or black necrosis with an irregular pale yellow border. In humid conditions, white cottony sporulation appears on the underside of infected leaves, and this is the key diagnostic sign requiring immediate action. Lesions expand visibly day by day and the entire plant can collapse within three to five days. A distinct musty or earthy odor is often noticed in heavily infected fields. On stems, dark brown to black lesions progress rapidly and cause complete stem death, collapsing the entire shoot above the infection point. On tubers, the disease produces reddish-brown discoloration beginning just beneath the skin, extending inward as a firm, dry rot with an irregular pattern. This is distinct from bacterial soft rots, which are watery and strongly odorous. Infected tubers in storage attract secondary bacterial invasion and can liquefy entirely. One infected tuber in a storage bin can spread disease to the entire lot.

Section: conditions
This section is about the favorable conditions for the disease.
Late Blight thrives in temperatures between ten and twenty-five degrees Celsius with an optimum around eighteen degrees. It requires greater than ninety percent relative humidity for at least twelve hours to produce spores effectively. Cool nights with dew and frequent rainfall are the classic warning conditions. Forecasting systems including the Hutton Criteria, which define infection risk as two consecutive days with minimum temperatures above ten degrees and relative humidity above ninety percent for at least six hours, are used by growers to anticipate outbreaks before symptoms appear.

Section: cycle
This section is about the disease cycle.
Primary inoculum comes from infected tubers overwintering in soil as volunteers, from unharvested cull piles, and from storage facilities. These sources begin sporulating under cool, wet spring conditions. Sporangia are dispersed by wind and rain to potato foliage. Zoospores or directly germinating sporangia infect through stomata and wounds. After an incubation of just two to four days, the plant begins sporulating itself, amplifying the inoculum exponentially. Rain washes sporangia into soil where zoospores infect tubers through lenticels. Infected tubers at harvest carry disease into storage, where it spreads to healthy lots. Where oospores form from sexual reproduction, they survive in soil for years and complicate rotation-based management.

Section: treatment
This section is about the treatment of the disease.
Planting only certified disease-free seed potatoes is the most fundamental preventive measure and is not optional. Infected seed is the most direct way to introduce the disease into a clean field. Resistant varieties with multiple resistance genes, particularly those with quantitative multigenic resistance, significantly reduce epidemic severity and are available through regional variety programs. Even resistant varieties require some fungicide protection in high-pressure seasons. Volunteer potato plants in and around fields serve as green bridges for the pathogen and must be destroyed. Cull piles must be buried deeply or composted far from fields immediately after sorting. Harvest should occur only when haulm is fully dead and tuber skins are fully set to minimize tuber infection during harvest operations.

Section: management
This section is about the management of the disease. Chemical control must use only oomycete-active fungicides. Protectant fungicides including chlorothalonil, mancozeb, and copper coat the tissue surface and prevent infection but have no curative activity. They must be reapplied frequently, especially after rain. Systemic fungicides with curative activity include dimethomorph, fluopicolide, mandipropamid, cymoxanil, and propamocarb. These penetrate tissue and can stop infections in progress for a limited window of twenty-four to forty-eight hours. Metalaxyl and mefenoxam are systemic but resistance is extremely common worldwide and they should never be used as solo products, only in premix with a contact fungicide. The spray program should begin preventively using forecasting criteria, not after symptoms appear. During high-risk weather, applications every five to seven days are required. An example rotation would cycle through mancozeb alone, then mandipropamid plus mancozeb, then chlorothalonil alone, then cymoxanil plus mancozeb, then fluopicolide plus propamocarb, repeating without ever using the same mode of action in consecutive applications.

Section: management
Storage management is as important as field management. All tubers should be carefully inspected before storage. Only healthy, dry, properly cured tubers should enter storage. Curing at twelve to fifteen degrees Celsius for ten to fourteen days allows wound healing before final storage temperature. Storage should be maintained at four to seven degrees Celsius with good ventilation and humidity around ninety percent. Regular inspection of stored lots is essential and any showing rot must be removed immediately before it spreads. In organic programs, copper-based fungicides are the primary chemical tool. Potassium phosphite provides systemic activity and some curative effect and is approved in some organic programs. Resistant varieties and extremely tight copper spray schedules of five to seven days during wet weather are the foundation of organic Late Blight management.

Section: actions
This section is about quick actions to take.
When disease is reported in the surrounding region, spray immediately without waiting for symptoms in your own field. When cool, foggy, or rainy weather is forecast, apply a systemic fungicide twenty-four hours before the weather event. When white mold appears on leaf undersides, this is an emergency requiring action within hours, not days. Remove all visibly infected plants, spray the entire field with a systemic plus protectant combination, and stop all overhead irrigation immediately. Alert neighboring farms because spores travel kilometers by wind. At harvest, inspect all tubers carefully and do not store any showing infection.

Section: scouting
This section is about monitoring and scouting.
Scout fields twice weekly from canopy closure through harvest during high-risk seasons. Focus first on field edges, low-lying areas, and areas near irrigation sources or volunteer plants. Check leaf undersides carefully because white sporulation appears there before upper surface symptoms are obvious. Use weather monitoring and disease forecasting tools to predict risk periods. Register for regional Late Blight alerts through local agricultural extension or plant health services. After any confirmed case is reported in the region, increase scouting to daily inspections.

Section: geography
This section is about the geographic distribution of the disease.
Late Blight is present on every continent where potatoes are grown. It is most severe in cool, wet climates including Ireland, the United Kingdom, northern Europe, the Pacific Northwest of the United States, the Andes region where the pathogen originated, and highland Africa. New, more aggressive genetic lineages including US-8 and several EU lineages have spread globally since the 1990s and have complicated resistance management in many regions.

Section: definition
This section is about the definition of the disease.
Late Blight is a disease caused by the oomycete pathogen Phytophthora infestans that attacks tomato and potato plants in cool, moist conditions. It is arguably the most historically significant plant disease in the world, being responsible for the Irish Potato Famine of the 1840s. On tomato, it is among the most destructive diseases possible, capable of destroying an entire field within days under ideal conditions. The disease affects all above-ground parts of the plant, causing large, rapidly expanding lesions on leaves, stems, and fruit that have a characteristic water-soaked, greasy appearance and, under humid conditions, a white downy sporulation on the underside of affected tissue.

Section: danger
This section is about the severity of the disease.
Late Blight is rated as the highest danger tomato disease. Under cool, moist conditions, it can destroy a tomato field completely within one to two weeks of first appearance. The speed of destruction is unmatched by any other common tomato pathogen. Yield losses of one hundred percent have been documented in unmanaged fields during epidemic years. The disease can spread from a single infected plant to an entire field within days during favorable weather, and it can spread between fields over distances of several kilometers via airborne sporangia.

Section: timeline
This section is about the timeline and spread speed of the disease.
The incubation period is three to seven days under optimal cool, moist conditions. Under highly favorable conditions of temperatures around fifteen to twenty degrees Celsius with prolonged leaf wetness, the disease can advance from first visible symptoms to complete field destruction within seven to fourteen days. The speed of spread is the defining characteristic of Late Blight epidemics and makes rapid response absolutely critical when the disease is first detected.

Section: spread
This section is about how the disease spreads.
Phytophthora infestans spreads primarily through airborne sporangia that are produced in massive quantities from infected tissue under cool, humid conditions. Sporangia can travel tens of kilometers on wind currents, making the disease capable of appearing in fields far from obvious inoculum sources. In the United States, the primary source of inoculum is infected transplants produced in southern growing regions and shipped north. The pathogen also survives in infected potato tubers left in soil from the previous season, which serve as a primary inoculum source in potato-growing regions. It requires free water on tissue surfaces for spore germination and infection.

Section: pathogen
This section is about the biology of the pathogen.
Phytophthora infestans is an oomycete, not a true fungus, though it resembles fungi in many ways. It is a diploid organism that produces both asexual sporangia and, when two compatible mating types are present, sexual oospores. The asexual sporangia can function as direct infection propagules or can release biflagellate zoospores under cool temperatures, each capable of independent infection. The pathogen is highly variable, with new genotypes arising through sexual recombination and selection. Fungicide resistance has developed in many populations to key fungicide classes.

Section: symptoms
This section is about the symptoms of the disease.
On leaves, Late Blight causes large, irregular, water-soaked lesions that appear greasy or dark green and expand rapidly. Under humid conditions, a white to grayish downy growth of sporangiophores and sporangia is visible on the underside of lesions. Lesions turn brown and papery as tissue dies. On stems, dark brown to black lesions cause rapid collapse and death of the stem above the infected area. Stem infections can kill the entire shoot within days. On fruit, firm, dark brown to grayish-green lesions develop that have a slightly greasy or water-soaked appearance. White sporulation may appear on fruit surfaces under humid conditions. Infected fruit collapse rapidly and become unmarketable.

Section: conditions
This section is about the favorable conditions for the disease.
Late Blight thrives in cool, moist conditions with temperatures between ten and twenty-four degrees Celsius, with optimum infection around fifteen to twenty degrees. The pathogen requires prolonged periods of leaf wetness for spore germination and infection. Foggy, cloudy weather with temperatures in this range is considered high risk. Hot, dry weather is inhibitory and can slow or stop epidemic development. The disease is less of a concern in hot, dry summer climates but can develop rapidly when cool periods with rain or heavy dews occur.

Section: cycle
This section is about the disease cycle.
The cycle begins with inoculum from infected transplants, potato tubers, or airborne sporangia from distant sources. Under cool, moist conditions, infected tissue rapidly produces sporangia that are dispersed by wind. New infections develop within three to seven days and produce the next generation of sporangia. This cycle repeats continuously under favorable conditions, allowing exponential increase in disease severity. Hot, dry weather interrupts the cycle. Sexual oospores, when present, allow long-term survival in soil.

Section: treatment
This section is about the treatment of the disease.
Prevention is the most important aspect of management, as the speed of Late Blight development makes reactive management very difficult. Using certified disease-free transplants from reputable sources is critical. Avoiding overhead irrigation and reducing leaf wetness periods limits infection events. Removing and destroying infected tissue immediately upon detection can slow spread within a field. No commercial tomato variety is fully resistant to all strains of Phytophthora infestans, but varieties with partial resistance or specific resistance genes such as Ph-3 provide meaningful reduction in disease severity and slow epidemic development significantly.

Section: management
This section is about the management of the disease.
Fungicide management must be preventive, as curative activity of even the best fungicides is very limited once Late Blight is established. Applications should begin when weather conditions become favorable or when Late Blight is detected in the region, whichever comes first, and not wait for disease to appear in the field. Fungicide classes with activity against oomycetes include mandipropamid, dimethomorph, fluopicolide, propamocarb, mancozeb, and copper compounds. Strobilurins do not have reliable activity against Phytophthora infestans. Applications should be made on five to seven day intervals during cool, wet weather. Resistance management is critical, as resistance to several key fungicide classes is widespread in many Phytophthora infestans populations.

Section: actions
This section is about quick actions to take.
When Late Blight is confirmed anywhere in the region, apply a preventive fungicide to all tomato fields immediately regardless of whether disease is visible. When first lesions appear in a field, immediately remove and destroy all infected tissue and apply a systemic oomycide within twenty-four hours. During cool, foggy weather, apply fungicides on five-day intervals. If disease cannot be controlled and is spreading rapidly, consider early harvest of marketable fruit before complete field destruction occurs.

Section: scouting
This section is about monitoring and scouting.
Scout fields daily during cool, moist weather and during regional Late Blight alerts. Examine all parts of the plant including lower leaves, stems, and fruit. During foggy or rainy cool periods, inspect with particular intensity as conditions can shift from low to high risk rapidly. Use regional Late Blight forecasting systems and alerts from extension services to anticipate high-risk periods. Confirm suspicious samples by laboratory diagnosis, as several other diseases can resemble Late Blight.

Section: geography
This section is about the geographic distribution of the disease.
Late Blight of tomato is most severe in cool, moist tomato-growing regions including the northeastern United States, northern Europe, the Andean regions of South America, and highland areas in tropical countries where cool nights and wet conditions favor the pathogen. It is a sporadic but catastrophic threat in all tomato-growing regions where cool, wet conditions can occur during the growing season.

Section: definition
This section is about the definition of the disease.
Isariopsis Leaf Blight, also known as Grape Leaf Blight, is a fungal disease of grapevines caused by Pseudocercospora vitis, previously known under the synonyms Isariopsis clavispora and Cercospora viticola. The disease primarily affects leaves, producing dark brown spots with a characteristic dark olive-colored sporulation on the underside that is its most reliable diagnostic feature. It causes progressive leaf yellowing and premature defoliation, weakening the vine as it enters winter dormancy. Isariopsis Leaf Blight is less aggressive and less economically important than Black Rot or Downy Mildew, but it can cause significant defoliation in warm, humid regions, particularly in tropical and subtropical grape-growing areas, and its impact accumulates over multiple seasons when management is neglected.

Section: danger
This section is about the severity of the disease.
Isariopsis Leaf Blight is rated as moderate danger. It does not kill the grapevine and rarely causes direct fruit loss because berry infection is uncommon. Its primary harm is through defoliation, which reduces the vine's ability to produce and store carbohydrates before winter dormancy. Vines repeatedly defoliated by this disease in late summer and autumn enter winter with reduced energy reserves, lower cold hardiness, and diminished vigor for the following season. The economic impact is lower than Black Rot or Downy Mildew but meaningful in tropical and subtropical regions where the disease is most prevalent.

Section: timeline
This section is about the timeline and spread speed of the disease.
The incubation period from infection to visible symptoms is fourteen to twenty-one days. The disease builds slowly through summer, reaching its peak intensity in late summer and autumn. Significant defoliation, when it occurs, develops over four to eight weeks. The progression is gradual compared to more aggressive diseases, which means that well-timed management interventions can effectively control the season's impact.

Section: spread
This section is about how the disease spreads.
Isariopsis Leaf Blight overwinters in infected fallen leaves on the vineyard floor, where the fungus survives as mycelium and conidia in the debris. In spring and early summer, warming temperatures and wet conditions stimulate spore production on this overwintered debris. Conidia are dispersed by wind and rain splash to the undersides of leaves, where they penetrate through stomata during periods of high humidity. Secondary infections occur throughout the growing season as new spore production from established lesions continues. The disease does not spread through soil, through root contact, or through pruning tools, unlike Esca. Infected leaves falling to the vineyard floor at the end of the season replenish the inoculum source for the following year.

Section: pathogen
This section is about the biology of the pathogen.
Pseudocercospora vitis is an ascomycete fungus that survives as mycelium and conidia in infected fallen leaf debris on the vineyard floor. It produces long, multiseptate conidia in olive-colored clusters called fascicles on the underside of infected leaves. The conidia are produced most abundantly during periods of high relative humidity and do not require rain for their formation, only humid air. Penetration occurs exclusively through stomata on the leaf underside. The fungus does not persist long-term in soil and is entirely dependent on leaf litter for overwintering. This dependency on leaf debris makes litter removal the most powerful single management intervention available.

Section: symptoms
This section is about the symptoms of the disease.
On leaves, Isariopsis Leaf Blight produces irregular, angular to rounded spots between two and fifteen millimeters in diameter. On the upper leaf surface the spots appear dark brown to reddish-brown with diffuse, sometimes unclear margins. On the lower leaf surface, directly beneath the spots, dark olivaceous sporulation appears, ranging from olive-green to black in color. This dark olive underside growth is the key diagnostic feature that distinguishes this disease from Downy Mildew, which produces white sporulation on the leaf underside. Affected lesions may coalesce into large blighted areas. Infected leaves turn yellow and drop prematurely, and defoliation can be substantial in severe cases during late summer and autumn. On petioles, dark elongated lesions can appear, occasionally causing premature leaf drop before the leaf itself shows complete symptoms. On berries, small dark spots occasionally form on the berry surface near harvest, but berry infection is uncommon and rarely causes significant direct fruit loss.

Section: conditions
This section is about the favorable conditions for the disease.
Isariopsis Leaf Blight thrives in temperatures between twenty and thirty degrees Celsius and under conditions of high relative humidity with frequent rainfall. Dense canopies with poor air circulation create the microclimate most favorable to the disease by trapping humidity near the leaf surface. The disease is most severe in late summer and autumn and is most prevalent in tropical and subtropical grape-growing regions where humidity remains high throughout the season.

Section: cycle
This section is about the disease cycle.
The fungus survives winter in infected leaf debris on the vineyard floor. When warm, moist conditions return in spring and early summer, the pathogen produces conidia on the debris that are dispersed by wind and rain splash to leaf undersides. Infection occurs through stomata during humid periods. After an incubation of fourteen to twenty-one days, symptoms develop and new conidia are produced on the underside of infected leaves. Secondary infections continue throughout the growing season. As the season advances into late summer and autumn, the disease intensifies and defoliation accelerates. Infected leaves fall to the ground and replenish the leaf litter inoculum pool for the following season.

Section: treatment
This section is about the treatment of the disease.
Removing and destroying fallen infected leaves after harvest is the single most effective management action for Isariopsis Leaf Blight. Because the disease depends entirely on leaf litter for overwintering, thorough leaf removal from the vineyard floor eliminates the primary inoculum source and directly reduces next season's disease pressure. Improving canopy airflow through shoot positioning, leaf removal, and appropriate training systems reduces the humidity microclimate that favors the disease. Drip or subsurface irrigation should be used in preference to overhead systems. Balanced fertilization with avoidance of excess nitrogen keeps canopy density manageable.

Section: management
This section is about the management of the disease.
This disease is usually managed as a secondary benefit of fungicide programs targeting the more serious diseases of Black Rot and Downy Mildew, because the fungicides used for those diseases, including copper, mancozeb, captan, azoxystrobin, and tebuconazole, are also effective against Pseudocercospora vitis. When Isariopsis Leaf Blight requires a dedicated chemical program, applications of copper or mancozeb every ten to fourteen days during wet periods provide adequate protection. Strobilurins and triazoles can be added when additional systemic activity is needed. Modes of action should be rotated to prevent resistance. In organic programs, copper-based fungicides applied preventively are the most reliable option. The emphasis should be on leaf litter removal and canopy management as the primary organic tools.

Section: actions
This section is about quick actions to take.
In early summer when no symptoms are yet visible, remove leaf litter from the previous season, improve canopy airflow, and apply a preventive copper spray if wet weather is forecast. When first spots appear on lower leaves, spray fungicide and remove heavily infected leaves. When significant defoliation is occurring, spray immediately and remove fallen leaves from the vineyard floor to reduce active inoculum. After harvest, collect and destroy all fallen leaves, because this is the intervention that most directly reduces the following year's disease pressure.

Section: scouting
This section is about monitoring and scouting.
Begin scouting in mid-summer when the disease typically starts to appear. Focus on lower canopy leaves where the disease initiates. Examine leaf undersides with a hand lens, looking for dark olive-colored conidiophore clusters that confirm Pseudocercospora vitis rather than another disease. Record the percentage of leaves with symptoms and estimate the percentage defoliation occurring before harvest. Monitor more intensively in humid, low-lying areas of the vineyard where air drainage is poorest.

Section: geography
This section is about the geographic distribution of the disease.
Isariopsis Leaf Blight occurs worldwide wherever grapevines are grown, but is most economically significant in Southeast Asia, South America, and parts of southern and eastern Europe. It is particularly prevalent in India, China, Japan, Brazil, and Argentina. In dry Mediterranean climates it causes less pressure, but warm, wet years can produce significant outbreaks even in drier regions.

Section: definition
This section is about the definition of the disease.
Leaf Mold is a fungal disease of tomato caused by Passalora fulva, formerly known as Cladosporium fulvum and also referred to as Fulvia fulva in some literature. It is primarily a disease of protected tomato production, most commonly occurring in greenhouses and high tunnels where humidity can rise and remain above the critical threshold of eighty-five percent relative humidity. Under those conditions the disease spreads rapidly and can strip all leaves from greenhouse plants within two to four weeks. In open-field production it is less common but can occur in dense plantings during prolonged wet and cloudy weather. The defining characteristic of the disease is an olive-green to grayish-purple velvety mold that forms on the underside of infected leaves, directly beneath pale yellow spots visible on the upper surface.

Section: danger
This section is about the severity of the disease.
Leaf Mold is rated as moderate danger in open fields and high danger in greenhouses and high tunnels. It does not kill plants directly, but complete defoliation severely weakens them and eliminates yield by removing the photosynthetic surface needed to fill fruit. Fruit infection is rare, making defoliation the primary economic concern. The disease is one of the leading causes of losses in protected tomato production worldwide and requires consistent humidity management to keep under control.

Section: timeline
This section is about the timeline and spread speed of the disease.
The incubation period from infection to visible symptoms is ten to fourteen days. In a greenhouse where humidity is consistently above eighty-five percent and ventilation is poor, the disease can spread through the entire structure in two to four weeks. In open-field conditions the spread is slower and more dependent on weather events. An important characteristic of the pathogen is that its conidia can survive on greenhouse structures including frames, plastic covering, stakes, and trellises for months to years between crop cycles, making them a persistent inoculum source for every new crop planted into an inadequately sanitized structure.

Section: spread
This section is about how the disease spreads.
The primary source in greenhouse production is infected plant debris and contaminated greenhouse surfaces where conidia survive between crops. At the start of each new crop cycle, humidity rises and dormant spores on structures become active and are carried by internal air currents from plant to plant. Workers handling infected plants transfer conidia on their hands, clothing, and tools, which is a significant pathway of spread especially in the early stages of an outbreak before the problem is recognized. Conidia germinate on the leaf underside and penetrate through stomata. The critical requirement is relative humidity above eighty-five percent. Below this threshold, conidia cannot germinate and the disease stops spreading regardless of how much inoculum is present. This single environmental threshold is the most important principle in managing Leaf Mold.

Section: pathogen
This section is about the biology of the pathogen.
Passalora fulva is an ascomycete fungus with high race diversity, with over thirty races identified based on their ability to overcome specific resistance genes in tomato called Cf genes. The sexual stage is rarely observed, and the disease is propagated almost entirely through asexual conidia. These conidia are produced in chains on conidiophores that emerge from stomata on the leaf underside, creating the visible velvety sporulation. The spores are highly resilient and remain viable on surfaces including metal, plastic, and wood for extended periods between crops. The fungus requires relative humidity above eighty-five percent both for spore germination and for new sporulation, which is why humidity control is simultaneously the most important monitoring parameter and the most effective management tool.

Section: symptoms
This section is about the symptoms of the disease.
On the upper leaf surface, Leaf Mold produces pale green to yellow spots of variable size with diffuse, irregular margins. These spots may be confused with nutrient deficiencies or other diseases unless the leaf underside is examined. On the lower leaf surface, directly beneath the upper surface spots, olive-green to grayish-purple velvety mold appears. This sporulation is the defining diagnostic feature of Leaf Mold. The color can range from olive-green early in the season to gray-brown as the season progresses, and can appear purplish under some conditions. This growth is the conidiophore production generating new infectious spores. Infected leaves curl at the edges, turn brown, and drop progressively from the lower to the upper canopy. In severe outbreaks, complete defoliation occurs within weeks. Stem and fruit infection are rare and only occur in very severe cases under extremely high humidity.

Section: conditions
This section is about the favorable conditions for the disease.
Relative humidity above eighty-five percent is the critical condition for Leaf Mold, both for spore germination and for new sporulation. Temperature between sixteen and twenty-four degrees Celsius, with an optimum around twenty-two degrees, is the most favorable range. Poor air circulation within a greenhouse creates local pockets of very high humidity that enable rapid spread even when average greenhouse humidity is somewhat lower. Overhead or foliar irrigation contributes directly to humidity and leaf wetness and accelerates the disease significantly.

Section: cycle
This section is about the disease cycle.
Conidia survive on greenhouse structures, in plant debris, and in soil between crop cycles. When a new crop is planted and canopy density increases, humidity rises above eighty-five percent and dormant conidia are activated.
