Air currents distribute spores through the greenhouse to leaf undersides. Conidia germinate and penetrate stomata during humid periods. After ten to fourteen days, new velvety sporulation appears on infected leaves and produces fresh conidia that air currents spread further. The cycle repeats rapidly as long as humidity remains above the threshold. At season end, conidia remain on greenhouse surfaces and in debris to initiate the cycle in the next crop.

Section: treatment
This section is about the treatment of the disease. Reducing relative humidity below eighty-five percent is the most important and most effective management action. This can be achieved by opening vents and running circulation fans, heating the greenhouse in the morning to reduce overnight condensation, reducing irrigation frequency, and avoiding all overhead or foliar watering. Below eighty-five percent relative humidity, sporulation stops and the disease ceases to spread regardless of inoculum levels, making environmental control more powerful than any chemical option. Plant spacing should be adequate to allow airflow through the canopy. Infected leaves should be removed promptly and disposed of outside the greenhouse rather than composted inside. Workers should implement hygiene protocols including changing clothes and washing hands before entering high-value or clean sections. Between crop cycles, thorough sanitization of the entire greenhouse is essential. All plant material must be completely removed.

Section: treatment
Surfaces including frames, plastic, stakes, trellises, and irrigation equipment should be washed with detergent to remove organic matter, then treated with bleach at one to ten dilution or quaternary ammonium compounds, and allowed to dry completely for at least one week before the next crop is transplanted. Resistant varieties carrying Cf resistance genes are available and provide effective protection against specific races of the pathogen. However, resistance is race-specific and breaks down when a new race matching the variety's Cf gene is present. Combining resistant varieties with humidity management provides the most reliable protection.

Section: management
This section is about the management of the disease.
Fungicide applications work significantly better when combined with humidity reduction and should not be relied upon as a substitute for environmental control. Effective fungicide classes include multi-site protectants such as chlorothalonil and mancozeb, strobilurins such as azoxystrobin, and triazoles such as difenoconazole. Applications every seven to ten days with thorough coverage of leaf undersides are required in active disease situations. Strobilurin resistance has been reported in some greenhouse populations, so modes of action should be rotated. In organic programs, copper-based fungicides and Bacillus subtilis products provide some suppressive activity. Humidity management and thorough greenhouse sanitization between crops are the primary organic tools.

Section: actions
This section is about quick actions to take.
When humidity is consistently above eighty-five percent, open all vents, run circulation fans, and reduce irrigation frequency immediately because this is the priority action before any chemical response. When first olive mold appears on leaf undersides, remove infected leaves immediately, spray fungicide with thorough underside coverage, and urgently increase ventilation. When disease is spreading rapidly through a greenhouse, combine emergency ventilation with plant removal in the worst areas and fungicide application throughout. Between crops, sanitize all greenhouse structures, stakes, and equipment before the next planting.

Section: scouting
This section is about monitoring and scouting.
Check plants two to three times per week in greenhouses throughout the growing season. Use a hygrometer to monitor humidity continuously and flag any periods when it consistently exceeds eighty-five percent. Scout during morning hours when sporulation is most visible. Check leaf undersides first because olive mold appears before upper surface yellowing becomes obvious. Record the percentage of plants affected and the severity of sporulation to track disease progression and the effectiveness of ventilation interventions.

Section: geography
This section is about the geographic distribution of the disease.
Leaf Mold is primarily a disease of protected tomato production and is most economically significant in major greenhouse tomato regions including the Netherlands, the United Kingdom, Scandinavia, Spain, Italy, Turkey, Japan, South Korea, and greenhouse production areas of North America. Open-field occurrence is limited to warm, humid coastal environments where sustained humidity above eighty-five percent is possible.

Section: definition
This section is about the definition of the disease.
Leaf Scorch is a fungal disease caused by Diplocarpon earlianum that attacks strawberry plants in cool, wet conditions. It is one of the most common foliar diseases of strawberry worldwide, causing small, irregular purple to reddish-purple spots on leaves that coalesce and give the leaf a scorched appearance. In severe cases, entire leaves are killed and plants are significantly weakened, reducing fruit yield and plant vigor. The disease is particularly problematic in matted row systems where plant density is high and canopy airflow is reduced, creating ideal conditions for fungal spread and infection.

Section: danger
This section is about the severity of the disease.
Leaf Scorch is rated as a moderate danger disease. It rarely causes complete crop failure in a single season but can cause significant reduction in plant vigor and productivity when infections are severe and repeated over multiple seasons. Perennial plantings that are not renewed frequently suffer cumulative damage that reduces yield over time. The disease is most economically significant in regions with cool, wet springs and summers that favor repeated infection cycles throughout the growing season.

Section: timeline
This section is about the timeline and spread speed of the disease.
The incubation period ranges from five to twelve days depending on temperature and humidity. The disease can produce multiple infection cycles throughout the growing season under favorable conditions. The most rapid spread occurs during cool, wet spring weather when young leaves are emerging and the pathogen has overwintered inoculum available. Summer infections continue at a slower rate but accumulate through the season.

Section: spread
This section is about how the disease spreads.
Diplocarpon earlianum overwinters on infected leaves and plant debris in the strawberry planting. In spring, rainfall triggers the release of conidia from overwintered lesions on old leaves. These spores are spread by rain splash to new leaves. Once established, new lesions produce conidia throughout the growing season that continue to spread through the planting during wet periods. Dense canopy plantings with poor airflow accumulate moisture and facilitate rapid secondary spread. Old infected leaves left in the planting are the primary source of inoculum season to season.

Section: pathogen
This section is about the biology of the pathogen.
Diplocarpon earlianum is an ascomycete fungus that produces both asexual conidia and sexual ascospores. The asexual stage is the primary means of spread during the growing season. The fungus sporulates within acervuli that form inside leaf lesions and release conidia during wet weather. The sexual stage provides overwintering persistence and genetic recombination. The fungus does not persist in soil but survives in infected plant tissue.

Section: symptoms
This section is about the symptoms of the disease.
On leaves, Leaf Scorch causes small, irregular spots that begin as reddish-purple flecks and enlarge to form spots with purplish-red margins and tan to gray centers. Individual spots range from two to five millimeters. As multiple spots merge, the leaf develops a scorched or blighted appearance with large areas of dead tissue surrounded by purple-red borders. Severely infected leaves dry out and die but often remain attached to the plant. On petioles and runners, small elongated lesions may develop that are less common but contribute to overall plant stress when severe. On fruit caps, reddish lesions may occasionally develop, though fruit infection is not the primary economic concern of this disease.

Section: conditions
This section is about the favorable conditions for the disease.
Leaf Scorch thrives in cool, wet conditions with temperatures between fifteen and twenty degrees Celsius for optimal infection. The fungus requires free water on leaf surfaces for spore germination and infection. Prolonged periods of leaf wetness from rain, dew, or overhead irrigation promote rapid disease development. Dense plantings that maintain high humidity within the canopy are particularly favorable. The disease is most severe in spring and early summer when conditions are cool and wet.

Section: cycle
This section is about the disease cycle.
The cycle begins with inoculum overwintering on infected leaves in the planting. Spring rains activate overwintered conidia that infect new leaves. Infected leaves develop lesions that produce new conidia within one to two weeks. Multiple cycles of infection and sporulation occur throughout the season during wet periods. At the end of the season, infected leaf tissue remains in the planting and serves as inoculum for the following spring.

Section: treatment
This section is about the treatment of the disease.
Cultural management is critical and begins with renovating matted row plantings after each harvest season. Renovation involving mowing, thinning, and narrowing of rows reduces canopy density, improves airflow, and removes heavily infected tissue. Removing old leaves and plant debris reduces overwintering inoculum. Avoiding overhead irrigation in favor of drip irrigation eliminates leaf wetness associated with irrigation. Proper plant spacing at establishment reduces canopy density in annual systems. Variety selection plays an important role. Some strawberry varieties such as Chandler and Jewel show moderate resistance, while others show high susceptibility. Selecting varieties with better resistance ratings reduces disease pressure in regions where Leaf Scorch is consistently problematic.

Section: management
This section is about the management of the disease.
Chemical control is justified in plantings with a history of the disease and during cool, wet seasons. Fungicide classes used include multi-site protectants such as captan and thiram, and single-site fungicides including triazoles such as myclobutanil and SDHI products. Applications should begin in early spring at leaf emergence and continue on ten to fourteen day intervals during favorable weather. Fungicide programs should be integrated with renovation practices for maximum effectiveness. Resistance management through mode of action rotation is important given the multiple generations of the pathogen produced each season.

Section: actions
This section is about quick actions to take.
After harvest, mow the planting, narrow the rows, and remove all old leaf tissue from the planting area. When first spots are detected in spring, apply a protective fungicide and increase monitoring frequency. During cool, wet spring weather, maintain ten-day spray intervals without gaps. When severe defoliation is observed in older plantings, assess whether renovation or replanting is more economical than continued management. Before planting new beds, select resistant varieties and certified disease-free transplants.

Section: scouting
This section is about monitoring and scouting.
Scout plantings weekly from early spring leaf emergence through the end of the growing season. Examine the older leaves in the lower canopy first, as disease typically appears there earliest. Assess the percentage of leaves with lesions and the severity of individual lesion development. Older matted row plantings should be scouted more intensively than new annual plantings. Monitor weather conditions for cool, wet periods that favor rapid disease increase.

Section: geography
This section is about the geographic distribution of the disease.
Leaf Scorch of strawberry is present in all major strawberry-producing regions worldwide. It is most severe in cool, humid regions with wet spring weather including the northeastern United States, northern Europe, and New Zealand. It is less damaging in warm, dry climates such as California where conditions do not favor prolonged leaf wetness.

Section: definition
This section is about the definition of the disease.
Northern Leaf Blight is a fungal disease caused by Exserohilum turcicum that attacks corn in cool, humid growing conditions. It is one of the most widespread and economically important foliar diseases of corn worldwide, capable of causing significant yield losses in susceptible hybrids under favorable weather conditions. The disease is characterized by large, cigar-shaped gray-green to tan lesions on the leaves that reduce photosynthetic capacity and, in severe cases, cause premature death of the upper canopy. It is most damaging when infection occurs before or during silking, as this is the period of maximum leaf area and grain fill demand.

Section: danger
This section is about the severity of the disease.
Northern Leaf Blight is rated as a moderate to high danger disease. Yield losses of ten to thirty percent are common in susceptible hybrids under favorable conditions, and losses exceeding fifty percent have been recorded in severe epidemics. The impact is greatest when the ear leaf and leaves above it become heavily infected before grain fill is complete. Early-season infection that reaches the upper canopy before tasseling is the most economically damaging scenario. The disease does not kill the corn plant outright but significantly reduces grain yield and test weight.

Section: timeline
This section is about the timeline and spread speed of the disease.
The incubation period ranges from seven to twelve days depending on temperature and humidity. The disease typically appears first on lower leaves and progresses upward through the canopy as the season advances. The most critical period for yield impact is from silking through grain fill. Under favorable conditions of cool temperatures and high humidity, the disease can spread rapidly through a susceptible corn stand within two to three weeks of initial infection.

Section: spread
This section is about how the disease spreads.
Northern Leaf Blight overwinters in corn residue on the soil surface. In spring and early summer, conidia are produced from infected residue and are carried by wind to developing corn plants. Initial infections on lower leaves produce abundant conidia within the lesions that continue to spread upward through the canopy via wind dispersal. The pathogen can spread within a field and between fields over significant distances. Continuous corn production and no-till practices that leave residue on the soil surface increase inoculum levels and disease risk.

Section: pathogen
This section is about the biology of the pathogen.
Exserohilum turcicum is a deuteromycete fungus that produces large, distinctive boat-shaped conidia with multiple septa. It overwinters primarily in infected corn residue, where it produces conidia during wet periods throughout the growing season. The fungus infects corn tissue by penetrating through stomata or directly through the cuticle. It requires free water on the leaf surface for spore germination and infection. Race variation within the pathogen population affects the effectiveness of host resistance genes in commercial hybrids.

Section: symptoms
This section is about the symptoms of the disease.
On leaves, Northern Leaf Blight produces large, elliptical to cigar-shaped lesions that are three to fifteen centimeters long. Young lesions are gray-green and water-soaked in appearance. As they mature, lesions become tan to grayish-brown with wavy margins that are not restricted by leaf veins. Under humid conditions, dark gray-green sporulation is visible within the lesions, giving them a dirty appearance. In severe cases, multiple lesions coalesce and kill entire leaves, causing premature senescence of the canopy. On husks and leaf sheaths, lesions may also develop but are less common and less economically significant than foliar infection.

Section: conditions
This section is about the favorable conditions for the disease.
Northern Leaf Blight thrives in temperatures between eighteen and twenty-seven degrees Celsius, with optimum infection around twenty-two degrees. The fungus requires six to eighteen hours of leaf wetness for successful infection, depending on temperature. Cool nights, morning dews, and frequent light rains create ideal conditions. The disease is most severe in years with cool, cloudy, humid weather during the growing season and in fields with heavy corn residue from no-till or continuous corn production.

Section: cycle
This section is about the disease cycle.
The cycle begins with conidia produced from infected corn residue in spring. These spores are windborne and infect young corn plants, initially causing lesions on lower leaves. Sporulating lesions produce additional conidia that spread upward through the canopy. The disease progresses most rapidly during periods of cool, humid weather. At the end of the season, infected crop residue falls to the soil surface and serves as the primary inoculum source for the following season.

Section: treatment
This section is about the treatment of the disease.
Cultural management begins with crop rotation away from corn for one to two years, which allows infected residue to decompose and reduces primary inoculum. Tillage to incorporate residue also reduces surface inoculum but must be balanced with soil health considerations. Selecting resistant hybrids is the most cost-effective and sustainable management strategy and is strongly recommended for fields with a history of Northern Leaf Blight or continuous corn production. Hybrid selection is the foundation of management. Many commercial corn hybrids carry resistance genes designated Ht1, Ht2, Ht3, or HtN that provide race-specific resistance. Non-race-specific quantitative resistance is also available in some hybrids and provides more durable protection. Fields with a history of the disease should use hybrids with the highest available resistance ratings.

Section: management
This section is about the management of the disease.
Fungicide applications are economically justified in susceptible hybrids when disease is present at or below the ear leaf at tasseling. The critical timing for fungicide application is at tasseling through silking, as this protects the ear leaf and upper canopy during grain fill. Fungicide classes used include triazoles such as propiconazole and tebuconazole, strobilurins such as azoxystrobin and pyraclostrobin, and premixed combinations of both classes. Single applications at tasseling are typically sufficient; a second application is rarely economically justified unless disease pressure is extreme.

Section: actions
This section is about quick actions to take.
Before planting, select hybrids with Northern Leaf Blight resistance ratings appropriate for fields with residue from previous corn crops. During the growing season, begin scouting at V6 and assess disease progress at tasseling. When lesions are found below the ear leaf at tasseling in a susceptible hybrid, apply a foliar fungicide immediately. When lesions reach the ear leaf or above before grain fill is complete, consider economic losses already incurred and adjust management for the following season.

Section: scouting
This section is about monitoring and scouting.
Scout fields weekly from V6 through silking. Examine leaves throughout the canopy, beginning with lower leaves where infection typically starts. Assess the vertical progress of the disease relative to the ear leaf and the growth stage of the crop. Fields with continuous corn, no-till, or a history of Northern Leaf Blight should be scouted most intensively. Make fungicide decisions at tasseling based on disease level at that timing.

Section: geography
This section is about the geographic distribution of the disease.
Northern Leaf Blight is most severe in the upper Midwest of the United States, the corn belt of Europe, and humid corn-producing regions of South America and Africa. It is present wherever corn is grown in cool, humid conditions and is less damaging in hot, dry climates where leaf wetness periods are short.

Section: definition
This section is about the definition of the disease.
Powdery Mildew of cherry is a fungal disease caused by Podosphaera clandestina that attacks cherry trees in warm, dry climates with moderate humidity. Unlike most fungal diseases, it does not require free water for infection and is therefore most problematic in regions with warm days, cool nights, and moderate humidity rather than rainy conditions. The disease affects leaves, shoots, and fruit, producing a characteristic white powdery coating of fungal mycelium and spores on affected tissue. It is one of the most common diseases of sweet cherry worldwide and is a significant concern in commercial cherry production.

Section: danger
This section is about the severity of the disease.
Powdery Mildew is rated as a moderate to high danger disease on cherry. It can cause significant economic losses through direct fruit infection, which results in premature cracking, surface russet, and poor appearance that reduces marketability. Heavy infection of young leaves and shoot tips can distort growth and reduce overall tree vigor. In seasons with favorable conditions, rapid spread through the canopy can occur within a few weeks. The disease is rarely fatal to established trees but has a substantial economic impact on fruit quality.

Section: timeline
This section is about the timeline and spread speed of the disease.
The incubation period is five to seven days under optimal conditions. Unlike most fungal diseases, Powdery Mildew can spread rapidly during warm, dry weather because its spores do not require water for germination. The most critical infection window for fruit is from petal fall through pit hardening. The disease can spread continuously throughout the growing season under favorable conditions, with multiple overlapping generations of spore production occurring without interruption.

Section: spread
This section is about how the disease spreads.
Powdery Mildew overwinters as chasmothecia in bark crevices and as dormant mycelium in infected buds. In spring, ascospores released from chasmothecia provide primary inoculum. Once established, the fungus produces abundant conidia from white powdery colonies on leaf surfaces. These dry conidia are windborne and spread rapidly throughout the canopy and to neighboring trees. Unlike most fungal pathogens, Powdery Mildew spores are actually inhibited by free water on leaf surfaces, making it uniquely adapted to spread during dry weather. A single infected shoot tip can produce millions of conidia within days.

Section: pathogen
This section is about the biology of the pathogen.
Podosphaera clandestina is an obligate biotrophic ascomycete that can only grow on living host tissue. It produces both a sexual stage with chasmothecia and an asexual stage with chains of conidia. The fungus grows on the surface of leaf tissue, penetrating only through specialized structures called haustoria that extract nutrients from epidermal cells. It does not penetrate deeply into plant tissue. The white powdery appearance results from the dense network of surface mycelium and conidial chains visible to the naked eye.

Section: symptoms
This section is about the symptoms of the disease.
On leaves, Powdery Mildew produces white to grayish-white powdery patches on both the upper and lower surfaces. Young leaves may become cupped, twisted, or stunted when heavily infected. Severely infected leaves may yellow and drop prematurely. On shoot tips, white powdery growth covers expanding tissue and causes stunting and distortion of new growth. Infected shoot tips may fail to harden properly before winter, increasing cold damage risk. On fruit, white powdery patches develop on the skin surface that cause russet discoloration after the mycelium is rubbed away. Infected fruit may crack prematurely and are unmarketable. Calyx-end infections are common.

Section: conditions
This section is about the favorable conditions for the disease.
Powdery Mildew thrives in temperatures between ten and thirty degrees Celsius, with the optimum around twenty to twenty-five degrees. It does not require free water for spore germination and is uniquely adapted to infect during dry weather. High relative humidity of sixty-five to eighty percent promotes sporulation without rain. Conditions of warm days and cool nights create ideal humidity conditions. Dense, shaded canopies with poor airflow favor disease development by maintaining the humidity levels needed for sporulation.

Section: cycle
This section is about the disease cycle.
The cycle begins with overwintering inoculum in bark and infected buds. Primary spores released in spring infect young emerging tissue. Infected tissue rapidly produces conidia that spread through the canopy. Multiple generations of conidia are produced throughout the growing season without interruption under favorable conditions. In late summer and autumn, sexual chasmothecia form on infected tissue and overwinter in bark crevices to begin the cycle again the following spring.

Section: treatment
This section is about the treatment of the disease.
Cultural management focuses on improving canopy airflow through pruning to reduce the humidity conditions that favor sporulation. Removing infected shoot tips during summer pruning reduces inoculum within the orchard. Avoiding excessive nitrogen fertilization reduces the flush of highly susceptible succulent growth. Resistant varieties should be selected where possible. Overhead irrigation should be avoided in favor of drip irrigation systems. Variety selection plays a significant role. Some sweet cherry varieties such as Bing show moderate susceptibility while others show higher resistance. Sour cherry varieties are generally less susceptible than sweet cherry varieties.

Section: management
This section is about the management of the disease.
Chemical control should begin at bud swell or at the first sign of disease and continue throughout the growing season during favorable conditions. Fungicide classes used include triazoles such as myclobutanil and tebuconazole, strobilurins such as azoxystrobin, SDHI products such as boscalid, and sulfur-based compounds in organic programs. Applications should be made on seven to fourteen day intervals during periods of active growth and favorable weather. Sulfur should not be applied when temperatures exceed thirty degrees Celsius to avoid phytotoxicity. Resistance management through mode of action rotation is essential given the continuous reproduction of the pathogen.

Section: actions
This section is about quick actions to take.
At bud swell, begin monitoring for first infections on emerging tissue. When first white colonies appear on shoot tips or leaves, apply a systemic fungicide immediately and increase monitoring frequency. During rapid shoot growth periods with warm days and cool nights, do not allow spray intervals to exceed seven days. When fruit infections are detected, prioritize fungicide coverage and remove heavily infected fruit clusters. After harvest, conduct summer pruning to remove infected shoot tips and improve canopy airflow.

Section: scouting
This section is about monitoring and scouting.
Scout weekly from bud swell through harvest, with particular attention to shoot tips and young leaves in the interior of the canopy. Assess the density of infected shoot tips after bloom as an indicator of disease pressure. Monitor weather conditions for warm days, cool nights, and moderate humidity that favor rapid spread. Interior canopy scouting is essential as these areas typically show disease first due to reduced airflow.

Section: geography
This section is about the geographic distribution of the disease.
Powdery Mildew of cherry is most severe in warm, semi-arid cherry-growing regions including the Pacific Northwest of the United States, southern Europe, and parts of Australia. It is present in all cherry-producing regions worldwide but is less problematic in rainy climates where free water on leaf surfaces inhibits spore germination.

Section: definition
This section is about the definition of the disease.
Septoria Leaf Spot is a fungal disease of tomato caused by Septoria lycopersici and is one of the most common tomato diseases in humid regions worldwide. It produces numerous small circular spots with gray centers and dark brown borders, and within each spot, tiny black dots are visible to the naked eye or under a hand lens. These black dots are pycnidia, the flask-shaped fruiting bodies of the fungus that release spores only when wet, and they are the definitive diagnostic feature that distinguishes Septoria from all other tomato leaf spot diseases. The disease begins on the oldest lower leaves after fruit set and moves steadily upward through the canopy. It rarely damages fruit directly, but severe defoliation exposes fruits to sunscald and weakens the plant's ability to fill them properly. Rain splash is the essential driver of spread, and keeping foliage dry through drip irrigation and mulching is highly effective at slowing the disease.

Section: danger
This section is about the severity of the disease.
Septoria Leaf Spot is rated as moderate to high danger. It rarely kills plants but heavy defoliation causes significant indirect damage through sunscald on exposed fruit, reduced photosynthesis, and weakened fruit fill. Yield losses in severe, unmanaged cases range from twenty to forty percent. The disease is most damaging when it progresses rapidly into the upper canopy before or during fruit fill. It is consistently among the most common reasons for tomato defoliation in the eastern United States and other humid regions.

Section: timeline
This section is about the timeline and spread speed of the disease.
The incubation period from infection to visible symptoms is five to ten days. Progression through the canopy from lower to upper leaves occurs over four to eight weeks in typical seasons. The disease usually appears after fruit set and intensifies through harvest. The speed of progression is moderate compared to diseases like Late Blight or Bacterial Spot, but it is steady and cumulative without management intervention.

Section: spread
This section is about how the disease spreads.
Septoria Leaf Spot overwinters in infected plant debris in the soil as pycnidia, which remain viable for two or more seasons. When rain or overhead irrigation wets the debris, pycnidia release spores that are splashed upward from the soil surface onto lower leaves. This rain splash mechanism is the primary driver of spread and the reason why mulching and drip irrigation are so effective at slowing the disease. Spores that land on leaves penetrate through stomata on the leaf underside during wet periods. Within the new lesions, pycnidia form rapidly and the cycle repeats with each rain event, progressively splashing spores higher into the canopy. Workers and equipment moving through wet fields spread spores laterally between plants and between fields. Infected transplants can also introduce the disease into clean fields.

Section: pathogen
This section is about the biology of the pathogen.
Septoria lycopersici is an ascomycete fungus that survives as pycnidia in infected plant debris in soil. The pycnidia are flask-shaped structures embedded in lesion tissue that release spores called pycnidiospores, which are long, filiform, and multiseptate. These spores are released only when the pycnidia are wetted by rain or irrigation, which is why the activation of spread is directly tied to rain events. The sexual stage of the fungus is called Mycosphaerella lycopersici but is rarely observed in practice. The pathogen is primarily a tomato pathogen but is also reported on eggplant, pepino, and some solanaceous weeds. It does not affect wheat or other cereals despite sharing the Septoria genus name with wheat pathogens, which are entirely unrelated organisms.

Section: symptoms
This section is about the symptoms of the disease.
On leaves, Septoria Leaf Spot produces numerous small, circular to slightly irregular spots between one and four millimeters in diameter. Each spot has a dark brown border surrounding a lighter gray or tan center. Inside the center of each spot, tiny black dots are visible. These are the pycnidia and are the definitive identification feature of Septoria Leaf Spot. The disease begins exclusively on lower, older leaves and moves upward through the canopy progressively over the season. In severe cases, spots coalesce into large irregular blighted areas and leaves turn yellow and drop. Defoliation can be complete on the lower and middle canopy in unmanaged crops. On stems and petioles, similar small spots with dark borders can form. Stem infection is less common but possible in wet conditions. Petiole lesions may cause premature leaf drop. On fruit, small spots with the same appearance as leaf lesions can rarely occur on green tomatoes. Fruit infection is uncommon and is not the main economic concern. The primary fruit-related problem is sunscald on exposed fruits after defoliation removes leaf cover.

Section: conditions
This section is about the favorable conditions for the disease.
Septoria Leaf Spot thrives in temperatures between twenty and twenty-five degrees Celsius. Extended wet periods with rain or overhead irrigation are essential for the splash-dispersal mechanism that drives spread. The disease is most severe after fruit set when plant tissue is aging and the canopy is dense. Overhead irrigation in warm weather produces ideal conditions for rapid progression through the canopy.

Section: cycle
This section is about the disease cycle.
The cycle begins with pycnidia in infected plant debris overwintering in the soil. When rain or irrigation wets the debris, pycnidia release spores that are splashed onto lower leaves. Spores penetrate through stomata during wet periods. After five to ten days, new lesions form with new pycnidia inside them. Repeated rain events splash spores progressively higher into the canopy. Workers and tools spread spores laterally between plants. At season end, infected debris falls to the soil and pycnidia persist there for one to two seasons, ready to initiate the next season's epidemic.

Section: treatment
This section is about the treatment of the disease.
Crop rotation away from tomato, eggplant, and solanaceous hosts for two or more seasons is the most effective long-term cultural tool because it allows pycnidia in the soil debris to degrade and substantially reduces the inoculum load for subsequent tomato crops. Organic mulch applied around the base of plants physically prevents rain splash from soil to lower leaves and is one of the most cost-effective early-season preventive measures available. Switching from overhead to drip irrigation eliminates the main driver of spore splash within the field and can dramatically slow disease progression on its own. Removing and destroying infected lower leaves as the disease progresses reduces the inoculum load in the canopy and improves airflow. Staking and training plants improves airflow through the canopy. Workers should avoid moving through fields when foliage is wet. All plant debris should be thoroughly removed and composted or buried after harvest.

Section: management
This section is about the management of the disease. Fungicide applications should begin when first symptoms appear or when weather consistently favors infection. Multi-site protectants including chlorothalonil and mancozeb provide no curative activity and must be applied preventively every seven to ten days. Copper-based products are effective and approved for organic use. Strobilurins such as azoxystrobin and pyraclostrobin provide protectant and some curative activity. Triazoles such as difenoconazole and propiconazole are systemic with curative activity within forty-eight to seventy-two hours of infection. SDHI products such as boscalid can be used in premix formulations. Applications every seven to ten days are typical, increasing to every five to seven days during wet weather. Thorough coverage of lower canopy leaves is important because this is where the disease initiates. Modes of action should be rotated with every two applications because strobilurin resistance in Septoria populations has been reported in some regions. In organic programs, copper-based sprays are the most reliable option.

Section: management
Bacillus subtilis products have some suppressive activity and can support an integrated program. Mulching and drip irrigation remain the most impactful organic tools.

Section: actions
This section is about quick actions to take.
When spots appear on only the lowest two to three leaves, remove the affected leaves, apply mulch, and begin a fungicide spray program. When disease is progressing into the mid-canopy near the fruit zone, spray a systemic fungicide immediately and stop overhead irrigation. When significant defoliation has occurred with fruit exposed to direct sun, apply fungicide and consider shade cloth for sun protection of fruit. When overhead irrigation is currently in use, switch to drip immediately because this single change can dramatically slow disease spread.

Section: scouting
This section is about monitoring and scouting.
Begin scouting at fruit set because the disease rarely appears before that stage. Focus on lower canopy leaves first because the disease always begins at the plant base. Use a hand lens at ten times magnification to confirm the tiny black pycnidia in lesion centers, which is the definitive identification feature. Record the percentage of plants affected, the highest leaf position showing disease, and the percentage of defoliation. Track disease progress weekly. Rapid upward movement signals the need for immediate intervention. Weather monitoring for periods of three or more consecutive days of temperatures between twenty and twenty-five degrees with rain represents high-risk windows requiring increased vigilance.

Section: geography
This section is about the geographic distribution of the disease.
Septoria Leaf Spot is present wherever tomatoes are grown in humid conditions. It is most severe in the eastern United States, northern and eastern Europe, parts of Latin America and the Caribbean, and South and Southeast Asia. It is less common in arid or semi-arid climates with little summer rainfall.

Section: definition
This section is about the definition of the disease.
Spider mites are microscopic arachnids, not insects, belonging to the family Tetranychidae. The most economically important species on tomato is Tetranychus urticae, the two-spotted spider mite, named for two dark spots visible on the yellowish-green body of adults. At approximately half a millimeter in body length, they are barely visible to the naked eye and require a ten-times hand lens for reliable identification. They feed by piercing individual leaf cells and extracting the cellular contents, creating a characteristic dusty, stippled appearance on leaves that is often the first sign of infestation. In hot, dry weather they reproduce with extreme speed, with one female capable of producing over one hundred eggs and a population capable of doubling every three to five days. Their arachnid nature is critical to management because standard insecticides have little or no effect on them and often make infestations dramatically worse by killing the predatory insects and mites that naturally keep spider mite populations in check.

Section: danger
This section is about the severity of the disease.
Spider mites are rated as high danger in hot, dry conditions. Severe infestations can kill tomato plants within two to three weeks during summer heat. They cause significant yield loss through defoliation and direct feeding damage on fruit. The speed of population increase is extreme in hot, dry weather, making early detection and rapid response essential. Resistance to multiple miticide classes is widespread in commercial production areas, complicating chemical control.

Section: timeline
This section is about the timeline and spread speed of the disease.
The life cycle from egg to adult takes seven to fourteen days at twenty-seven degrees Celsius and as little as five days at thirty-eight degrees. A single female produces over one hundred eggs in her lifetime. The population doubles every three to five days under hot, dry conditions, meaning that a small, early infestation can become a catastrophic outbreak within two weeks if not addressed. Within a field, mites spread by ballooning on silk threads carried by wind and can move through an entire field in one to two weeks. Cool, humid weather drastically slows reproduction and allows natural enemies to recover.

Section: spread
This section is about how the disease spreads.
Spider mites overwinter as adult females, which turn reddish-orange in color, in soil, plant debris, and weeds around the field. They emerge when temperatures exceed twelve to fifteen degrees Celsius in spring and begin feeding and reproducing on available plant material. Wind carries tiny mites on silk threads from plant to plant and from field to field, which is the main mechanism of long-distance spread. Workers and equipment carry mites on clothing and tools within and between operations. Infected transplants can introduce mites directly into a clean field at planting. Weeds in and around field margins serve as persistent reservoirs for spider mite populations throughout the season. The most common cause of severe outbreaks in commercial production is the use of broad-spectrum insecticides such as pyrethroids, which kill the predatory insects and mites that naturally regulate spider mite populations while having little effect on the mites themselves.

Section: pathogen
This section is about the biology of the pathogen.
Tetranychus urticae is a member of the class Arachnida, possessing eight legs as an adult, compared to the six legs of insects. This biological distinction explains why insecticides, which are designed to affect insect-specific biological processes, have limited efficacy against spider mites. The two dark spots on adults are accumulated gut contents rather than pigmentation. Adults live approximately three to four weeks and females lay one hundred to two hundred eggs during their lifetime. Eggs are laid on the underside of leaves and are spherical, pale, and shiny. Larvae hatch with six legs and transition through two nymphal stages before reaching the eight-legged adult form. The entire life cycle can be completed in five days at thirty-eight degrees Celsius, which explains why populations explode so dramatically during heat waves. Over twenty generations can occur in a single growing season. The species has an extremely broad host range, infecting over eleven hundred plant species across more than one hundred and forty plant families.

Section: symptoms
This section is about the symptoms of the disease.
Early infestations produce tiny yellow or white stippling dots on the upper leaf surface, each dot representing a single cell drained by mite feeding. This stippling may initially be confused with thrips damage or nutrient deficiency, and confirmation requires checking the leaf underside for the mites themselves. As populations grow, the stippling coalesces and leaves develop a bronze, silver, or dusty overall appearance from the accumulated cell damage across large areas of the leaf surface. Heavy infestations produce fine silk webbing on leaf undersides and between leaves. This webbing confirms a large, established colony and indicates that populations are already at damaging levels. Leaves under heavy webbing turn entirely yellow and then brown and die, and the plant defoliates progressively from the bottom up. In the most severe cases during summer heat, plants can collapse and die within two to three weeks of the infestation becoming established.

Section: conditions
This section is about the favorable conditions for the disease.
Hot temperatures between twenty-seven and thirty-eight degrees Celsius dramatically accelerate reproduction. Low humidity below fifty percent suppresses natural enemies and allows mite populations to grow unchecked. Drought-stressed plants are significantly more susceptible than well-watered plants because plant defenses are compromised and stressed tissue provides more concentrated nutrients. The disease is primarily a summer problem in field production and a year-round problem in greenhouses. The use of broad-spectrum insecticides is itself a major favorable condition because it eliminates natural biological controls.

Section: cycle
This section is about the disease cycle.
Overwintering adult females emerge from soil and debris in spring when temperatures exceed twelve to fifteen degrees Celsius. They begin laying eggs on leaf undersides immediately. Eggs hatch within three to five days at warm temperatures. Larvae feed and develop through two nymphal stages to reach adulthood within seven to fourteen days at twenty-seven degrees Celsius. Adults mate and females begin laying new eggs within hours of reaching maturity. As populations become dense, mites produce silk and disperse by ballooning on air currents to new plants. In late season and autumn, mated females develop the reddish-orange winter coloration and move to overwintering sites in soil, bark crevices, and plant debris.

Section: treatment
This section is about the treatment of the disease. Maintaining consistent, adequate irrigation is one of the most important cultural practices for spider mite management because drought-stressed plants are far more susceptible and recover more slowly from feeding damage. Well-watered plants show greater tolerance and support faster recovery after mite populations are brought under control. Preserving natural enemies is the single most important practice for long-term spider mite management. Avoiding broad-spectrum insecticides including pyrethroids, organophosphates, and carbamates preserves the populations of predatory mites and insects that naturally regulate spider mite numbers. Eliminating these natural controls is the primary cause of serious mite outbreaks in commercial production. Controlling weeds around field margins removes the reservoir populations that supply mites to the crop throughout the season. Removing the most heavily infested plant material reduces the population load. Reflective silver mulches on the soil surface disorient and deter mite colonization of plants.

Section: treatment
Biological control using predatory mites is the preferred approach in integrated pest management programs. Phytoseiulus persimilis is a highly effective specialist predator of spider mites that requires spider mite prey to survive. Neoseiulus californicus is more heat-tolerant and can persist at lower prey densities, making it better for preventive releases. Amblyseius andersoni is very heat-tolerant and performs well in summer conditions. Combining Phytoseiulus persimilis for fast knockdown with Neoseiulus californicus for persistence provides the best results. Releases should be made at the first sign of infestation and no miticides should be applied after release for a minimum of four to six weeks.

Section: management
This section is about the management of the disease. Chemical control requires miticides or acaricides specifically labeled for spider mites. Standard insecticides must not be used. Abamectin is highly effective and works as a neurotoxin but has limited ovicidal activity. Spiromesifen has excellent activity against eggs and larvae but no adult knockdown and must be applied when eggs and juveniles are present. Etoxazole and hexythiazox are mite growth inhibitors effective against eggs and larvae but not adults and must be used early in the infestation. Fenpyroximate provides fast adult knockdown but no ovicidal activity. Bifenazate has good adult knockdown. These chemistries must be rotated with every application using the IRAC resistance group numbers as a guide because resistance develops extremely rapidly in spider mite populations. Two sequential applications five to seven days apart are needed to catch eggs hatching after the first treatment. Thorough coverage of leaf undersides is mandatory because that is where mites live and feed and top-only spraying is ineffective.

Section: management
Horticultural oil and insecticidal soap are contact killers that suffocate mites and eggs on direct contact, require excellent coverage, and are suitable for organic programs. They have no residual activity.

Section: actions
This section is about quick actions to take.
When first stippling appears on a few leaves, apply predatory mites if available and apply neem oil or insecticidal soap as a bridge treatment. When bronze or silver leaves with webbing are present, apply a miticide immediately with thorough underside coverage and check the entire field for spread. When hot, dry weather above thirty degrees Celsius coincides with any stippling, act immediately because populations will double every three to five days in that weather. After any broad-spectrum insecticide application, expect a mite outbreak and monitor daily. When plants are heavily infested, remove and destroy the worst plants and treat surrounding plants with both miticide and predatory mite releases.

Section: scouting
This section is about monitoring and scouting.
Scout weekly from transplanting and increase to twice weekly during hot, dry weather above twenty-seven degrees Celsius. Sample a minimum of thirty leaves per field from multiple locations throughout the field. Focus on older mid-canopy leaves and field margins where infestations typically start. Use a ten-times hand lens to count motile mites per leaf. An economic threshold of five to ten motile mites per leaf is commonly used for field crops as a trigger for treatment. In greenhouses, treat when mites are first detected on ten percent of plants. Check for the presence of predatory mites before deciding on chemical treatment. If predatory mites are active and populations are not increasing, it may be possible to hold off on chemical applications and allow biological control to take effect.

Section: geography
This section is about the geographic distribution of the disease.
Spider mites are present globally on every continent where crops are grown. They are a major pest in Mediterranean Europe, the Middle East, North Africa, the United States, Latin America, and Southeast Asia. They are a year-round problem in tropical and subtropical regions and a seasonal summer problem in temperate climates. In greenhouses, populations are present year-round regardless of outdoor climate.

Section: definition
This section is about the definition of the disease.
Target Spot is a fungal disease of tomato caused by Corynespora cassiicola, a pathogen with one of the broadest host ranges of any plant pathogen, infecting over four hundred plant species across more than one hundred families. On tomato it produces circular to irregular brown lesions with concentric rings resembling a target or bullseye on leaves, stems, and fruit. Unlike Early Blight, which almost exclusively attacks older lower leaves, Target Spot infects leaves of all ages simultaneously, which is one of the key features distinguishing it in the field. It also infects fruit directly, a characteristic Early Blight lacks on tomato, making Target Spot capable of more direct economic damage to the marketable product. The disease has been increasing in importance globally over the past two decades, particularly in greenhouse production and tropical regions, partly due to the wide host range that provides persistent inoculum from many crop and non-crop sources in the environment.

Section: danger
This section is about the severity of the disease.
Target Spot is rated as moderate to high danger. It does not kill plants but severe defoliation combined with direct fruit infection can cause yield losses of twenty to fifty percent in severe cases. The damage from fruit lesions is particularly significant economically because it directly affects the marketable product. The disease is increasing in importance and was previously considered minor but is now recognized as a serious threat in many production systems worldwide.

Section: timeline
This section is about the timeline and spread speed of the disease.
The incubation period from infection to visible symptoms is five to seven days. The disease can affect plants at any growth stage and accumulates progressively over weeks. Fruit infection is most damaging when it occurs on green, expanding berries. The speed of spread is moderate but broader than many tomato diseases because the wide host range means inoculum is consistently available from multiple sources.

Section: spread
This section is about how the disease spreads.
Target Spot overwinters in infected plant debris in the soil as conidia and mycelium. Conidia are released during wet weather and dispersed by wind and rain splash to leaves, stems, and fruit. The fungus penetrates through stomata or directly through the cuticle and can infect tissue of any age, unlike Septoria and Early Blight which preferentially infect older tissue. Between plants, wind-dispersed conidia carry the disease throughout the crop. The extremely wide host range of Corynespora cassiicola means that inoculum can originate from neighboring crops, ornamental plants, and weeds in addition to infected tomato debris. This makes inoculum management more complex than for tomato-specific pathogens because eliminating the disease from tomato debris does not eliminate the environmental inoculum pool. In greenhouses, workers and tools spread conidia and high humidity enables rapid spread between plants.

Section: pathogen
This section is about the biology of the pathogen.
Corynespora cassiicola is an ascomycete fungus producing very distinctive conidia that are extremely large, up to five hundred micrometers long, multicellular, and cylindrical with a long tapering beak. Under a microscope the conidia are unmistakable and confirm identification definitively. The sexual stage is rarely observed in practice and the disease propagates almost entirely through asexual conidia. The pathogen survives in infected plant debris in soil, though its persistence is shorter than some other debris-inhabiting pathogens. Pathogenic races have been described based on virulence differences, though the race structure is less well characterized than in many other pathogens. Resistance to benzimidazole fungicides such as carbendazim has been documented in populations worldwide, and strobilurin resistance has also been reported, making fungicide rotation critical.

Section: symptoms
This section is about the symptoms of the disease. On leaves, Target Spot produces circular to slightly irregular lesions with distinct concentric rings giving the target or bullseye appearance. Lesion size typically ranges from one to two centimeters and can be larger, which is substantially bigger than Septoria lesions. The color is brown to dark brown with a yellow halo. Unlike Early Blight which mainly affects older lower leaves, Target Spot infects leaves at all ages and positions in the canopy simultaneously. In advanced cases a gray center develops in lesions as the fungus sporulates on the dead tissue. Severe infection causes complete defoliation beginning in the lower canopy and progressing upward. On fruit, Target Spot produces dark, sunken, circular lesions with the same concentric ring pattern seen on leaves. These lesions can penetrate into the flesh and create entry points for secondary bacterial and fungal rots. Significant direct fruit loss is possible, which distinguishes Target Spot from Early Blight and Septoria that rarely infect tomato fruit. On stems and petioles, dark elongated lesions form and can girdle tissue causing dieback.

Section: symptoms
Stem cankers are more commonly observed in greenhouse production where plants are larger and stem tissue is more exposed.

Section: conditions
This section is about the favorable conditions for the disease.
Target Spot thrives in temperatures between twenty-five and thirty-two degrees Celsius with an optimum around twenty-eight degrees. High humidity and extended leaf wetness periods are required for infection. The disease is most severe during warm, wet periods and in greenhouse and high tunnel production where high humidity is common. Dense canopies and overhead irrigation create ideal conditions for rapid spread.

Section: cycle
This section is about the disease cycle.
The cycle begins with conidia and mycelium surviving in infected plant debris in soil. Conidia are released during wet weather and dispersed by wind and rain splash. The fungus infects tissue of any age through stomata or direct cuticle penetration during humid periods. After five to seven days, new conidia are produced on lesion surfaces and wind and rain carry them throughout the crop. Fruit infection occurs throughout the season when conditions are favorable. At season end, infected debris returns to the soil to initiate the next season's epidemic. Because of the wide host range, conidia from non-tomato hosts in the environment contribute continuously to the inoculum pool.

Section: treatment
This section is about the treatment of the disease.
Crop rotation reduces debris-based inoculum but is less effective than for tomato-specific pathogens because the broad host range means inoculum can originate from many non-tomato sources. Rotating away from cucumbers, soybeans, and other highly susceptible crops is also worthwhile. Removing and destroying infected plant debris after harvest breaks the debris-based inoculum cycle. Improving air circulation through canopy management, pruning, and adequate plant spacing reduces the humidity microclimate favorable to infection. Drip irrigation should replace overhead systems to keep foliage dry. Infected lower leaves should be removed as disease progresses to reduce canopy inoculum. Surrounding crops and weeds should be scouted and managed to reduce alternate host sources of inoculum near the field. Greenhouse structures should be sanitized thoroughly between crops. Truly resistant commercial tomato varieties are currently limited and active research is ongoing. Check regional variety trial data for tolerance ratings as new varieties become available.

Section: management
This section is about the management of the disease. Fungicide applications should be applied preventively when conditions favor infection or at the appearance of first symptoms. SDHI products including boscalid, fluopyram, and penthiopyrad provide excellent activity and are among the most effective options available. Strobilurins such as azoxystrobin and pyraclostrobin provide good protectant activity but resistance has been reported. Triazoles such as difenoconazole and tebuconazole are systemic with curative activity. Multi-site protectants including chlorothalonil and mancozeb are important components of any program for resistance management. Benzimidazoles such as carbendazim should be avoided or minimized because resistance is documented globally. Application intervals of seven to ten days are typical, shortening to five to seven days during wet weather. Good coverage of the entire plant including fruit clusters and stem tissue is important. Modes of action must be rotated with every application to prevent resistance. A practical rotation would cycle through chlorothalonil, then SDHI plus triazole premix, then strobilurin plus mancozeb, then back to chlorothalonil.

Section: management
In organic programs, copper-based fungicides provide moderate protectant activity. Bacillus subtilis products have some suppressive activity. Cultural controls including rotation, canopy management, drip irrigation, and debris removal are the foundation of organic Target Spot management.

Section: actions
This section is about quick actions to take.
When bullseye spots appear on leaves of all ages rather than only old lower leaves, apply a systemic fungicide immediately and remove severely infected leaves to confirm Target Spot rather than Early Blight. When fruit shows circular sunken lesions, spray immediately with an SDHI or strobilurin product and remove infected fruit. When disease appears in a greenhouse with high humidity, improve ventilation urgently, apply fungicide, and remove infected tissue. When disease is appearing simultaneously in multiple crop types nearby, scout for alternate host sources and consider expanding the spray program coverage.

Section: scouting
This section is about monitoring and scouting.
Begin scouting soon after transplanting because Target Spot can appear at any plant stage. Check leaves of all ages at every scouting visit, because unlike Early Blight or Septoria, young leaves are also susceptible and this is the key field diagnostic feature. Inspect fruit clusters carefully because fruit lesions may appear before canopy defoliation becomes severe. Record the percentage of plants affected, which leaf ages are affected, and the percentage of fruit showing lesions. In greenhouses, pay attention to stem lesions at plant base and mid-stem. High-risk weather periods are temperatures between twenty-five and thirty-two degrees Celsius combined with overhead irrigation, rain, or dense fog.

Section: geography
This section is about the geographic distribution of the disease.
Target Spot was originally considered a tropical and subtropical disease but has expanded in importance globally, particularly in protected production environments. It is now significant in Southeast Asia, sub-Saharan Africa, the Caribbean and Latin America, the southern United States including Florida and Gulf Coast states, Mediterranean greenhouse production, and northern European greenhouse cucumber and tomato production.

Section: definition
This section is about the definition of the disease.
Tomato Mosaic Virus is a viral disease caused by Tomato mosaic virus, a member of the Tobamovirus genus, that infects tomato plants worldwide. It is one of the most stable and persistent plant viruses known, capable of surviving for years in dried plant debris, on contaminated tools, and on worker hands and clothing. The disease causes mottling, mosaic patterning, and distortion of leaves, reduces plant vigor, and can cause fruit deformation and color abnormalities that reduce marketability. It spreads primarily through mechanical contact rather than insect vectors, making field hygiene and contamination prevention the central elements of management.

Section: danger
This section is about the severity of the disease.
Tomato Mosaic Virus is rated as a moderate to high danger disease. Yield losses of ten to twenty-five percent are common in infected fields, and losses can exceed fifty percent when infection occurs early in the growing season and affects a high proportion of plants. The virus reduces photosynthetic efficiency, distorts fruit development, and can cause internal browning of fruit tissue that renders it unmarketable even when external appearance seems acceptable. The extraordinary persistence of the virus in the environment makes it particularly challenging to eradicate once a production facility is contaminated.

Section: timeline
This section is about the timeline and spread speed of the disease.
Tomato Mosaic Virus spreads extremely rapidly through mechanical contact, potentially reaching every plant in a greenhouse within hours if proper precautions are not taken. In the field, spread occurs more slowly through worker movement and equipment but can still progress through a planting within days during transplanting or pruning operations. The speed of spread is directly correlated with the number of human contacts made with infected and then healthy plants.

Section: spread
This section is about how the disease spreads.
Tomato Mosaic Virus spreads primarily through mechanical contact, which distinguishes it from most other plant viruses that require insect vectors. The virus is present in infected plant sap and is transmitted when contaminated hands, tools, clothing, or equipment contact healthy plant tissue through wounds, stomata, or trichome damage during normal handling. It can survive in dried plant tissue for years, in tobacco products for extended periods, and on contaminated surfaces in greenhouses for weeks to months. Seed transmission occurs at low rates from infected plants. The virus does not spread through soil or water and does not require an insect vector for spread.

Section: pathogen
This section is about the biology of the pathogen.
Tomato mosaic virus is a positive-sense single-stranded RNA virus with a rod-shaped virion structure characteristic of the Tobamovirus genus. It is closely related to Tobacco mosaic virus and shares many biological characteristics including extraordinary environmental stability. The virus replicates in the cytoplasm of infected cells and moves systemically through the phloem to infect all parts of the plant. It produces very high concentrations of virus particles in infected tissue, making even small amounts of infected sap highly infectious. The virus does not require a biological vector for transmission.

Section: symptoms
This section is about the symptoms of the disease.
On leaves, Tomato Mosaic Virus causes a distinctive mosaic or mottling pattern of light and dark green areas that follow a non-uniform distribution across the leaf blade. Leaves may show various degrees of distortion, including curling, bubbling, or narrowing depending on strain and plant age at infection. On young plants infected early, severe stunting and distortion of the entire plant may occur. Growing points may show light green to yellow coloration and distorted leaf development. On fruit, external symptoms include uneven ripening with green or yellow patches on otherwise ripe fruit. Internal browning of fruit wall tissue, visible when fruit is cut, can occur with some virus strains and renders fruit commercially worthless despite normal external appearance.

Section: conditions
This section is about the favorable conditions for the disease.
Tomato Mosaic Virus does not have specific weather-related favorable conditions for spread the way fungal and bacterial pathogens do, because it does not require moisture for transmission. Spread is driven by human activity: transplanting, pruning, trellising, harvesting, and other operations that involve repeated plant contact. Greenhouses and high-density production systems where workers contact many plants rapidly are particularly high-risk environments. Operations that employ workers who use tobacco products face additional risk due to virus survival in tobacco.

Section: cycle
This section is about the disease cycle.
Unlike fungal and bacterial diseases, Tomato Mosaic Virus does not have a seasonal cycle driven by weather. Once introduced into a planting, infected plants remain infected and serve as continuous inoculum sources throughout the season and potentially longer through contaminated debris. The virus persists in the environment between crops and can reinfect new plantings from contaminated surfaces, tools, and debris. Seed from infected plants can introduce the virus to new, otherwise clean production sites.

Section: treatment
This section is about the treatment of the disease.
There is no curative treatment for virus-infected plants. Management is entirely based on prevention and containment. Confirmed infected plants should be removed and destroyed immediately to reduce inoculum within the field or greenhouse. Roguing infected plants is most effective when done early, before secondary spread has occurred. Variety selection is the most effective long-term management strategy. Many commercial tomato varieties carry the Tm-2 or Tm-22 resistance gene that provides good to excellent resistance to most strains of Tomato Mosaic Virus. These resistance genes have been incorporated into most commercial tomato breeding programs and are widely available.

Section: management
This section is about the management of the disease.
Management of Tomato Mosaic Virus relies entirely on prevention and sanitation. Key practices include using certified virus-free seed or hot water-treated seed, selecting resistant varieties, disinfecting all tools and equipment between plants during pruning and harvesting operations using ten percent bleach solution or other registered disinfectants, requiring workers to wash hands with soap and water before entering the crop and between rows, prohibiting tobacco use in or near the greenhouse or field, removing and destroying infected plants immediately, and disinfecting greenhouses between crops. No chemical treatment has any activity against the virus in infected plants.

Section: actions
This section is about quick actions to take.
When mosaic symptoms are first detected, immediately isolate suspected infected plants and submit samples for laboratory confirmation. Upon confirmation, remove and bag infected plants without contacting other plants and destroy them outside the production area. Disinfect all tools, work surfaces, and worker hands and clothing. Identify the probable source of introduction and correct the practice or condition that allowed it. Increase roguing intensity to identify additional infected plants before further spread occurs.

Section: scouting
This section is about monitoring and scouting.
Scout plants weekly from transplanting through harvest, with particular attention at transplanting when mechanical spread risk is highest. Examine growing points and young leaves for mosaic patterns, distortion, and color abnormalities. In greenhouses, walk rows in a systematic pattern and mark any suspicious plants for follow-up observation or sampling. Maintain records of where infected plants are found to identify patterns that suggest the source or route of spread.

Section: geography
This section is about the geographic distribution of the disease.
Tomato Mosaic Virus is present in all tomato-producing regions worldwide. It is most problematic in greenhouse production systems where dense plant populations and frequent human contact create ideal conditions for mechanical spread. It is a universal threat wherever tomatoes are grown, with no geographic region being free from risk where contaminated seed, transplants, or production equipment are present.

Section: definition
This section is about the definition of the disease.
Tomato Yellow Leaf Curl Virus is a viral disease caused by Tomato yellow leaf curl virus, a member of the Begomovirus genus transmitted by the silverleaf whitefly, Bemisia tabaci. It is one of the most destructive viral diseases of tomato in tropical and subtropical regions worldwide, capable of causing complete crop loss when infection occurs early in the season. The disease causes severe stunting, leaf curling and yellowing, and flower drop that results in negligible fruit production in heavily infected plants. Unlike mechanically transmitted viruses, TYLCV requires the whitefly vector to spread, making whitefly management the central element of disease control.
